hard_prompt:
In this task, you're given a fill-in-the-blank question that contains two object names. Additionally, you're given one answer which is one of the objects present in the question. In this task, you need to minimally change the given question so that the answer flips to another object in the question. Remember the question has to be about both objects which are related but different; for example, "trophy" and "suitcase". This task typically involves replacing one word i.e., the 'trigger word' with its antonym (e.g., changing from "small" to "big"). You should not change any content in the given question beyond a word or two, i.e., the trigger word/phrase. The expected answer to your question must not be associated with just the trigger word; instead, it should depend on the context present in the question. The expected answer should not be ambiguous. For your question, there should be an agreed upon answer to fill in the blank. Your generations should NOT contain potentially explicit, offensive, or adult content. In your generated question, retain the object names from the input question. Avoid repeating the same style or phrase in generating your modified question e.g. this task can be always solved using a simple negation i.e. by adding not, never, etc. Instead, try to increase the word diversity. Your question must contain at least 15 and at most 30 words. Your question must have at least 70% overlapping words with the given question. You must utilize the given context word while writing the question. Your question must contain only one blank. The two objects should be used ONCE in your question. instances.input:
Sentence1: Some parasites kill their host, but most do not. 
Sentence2: All parasites are harmful to their host, but some are beneficial to humans.
instances.inspect_output:
The two sentences present different perspectives on the relationship between parasites and their hosts. 

Sentence 1 states that while some parasites can kill their hosts, the majority do not have such lethal effects. This implies that most parasites coexist with their hosts without causing death.

Sentence 2, on the other hand, asserts that all parasites are harmful to their hosts, but it also introduces the idea that some parasites can be beneficial to humans. This suggests a more complex relationship where parasites may have negative effects on their hosts but can also provide benefits in certain contexts.

In summary, Sentence 1 focuses on the lethal potential of parasites, while Sentence 2 emphasizes the harmful nature of parasites with a note on potential benefits to humans.
instances.control_output:
Sentence1: Some parasites kill their host, but most do not.  
Sentence2: All parasites are harmful to their host, but some are beneficial to humans.

hard_prompt: In this task, you're given a paragraph from the research paper and your task is to generate a suitable title for the research paper based on the given paper. Under 100 words is a good title length.

instances.input: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is characterized by accumulation of a surfactant-like substance in alveolar spaces and hypoxemic respiratory failure. Genetic PAP (GPAP) is caused by mutations in genes encoding surfactant proteins or genes encoding a surfactant phospholipid transporter in alveolar type II epithelial cells. GPAP is also caused by mutations in genes whose products are implicated in surfactant catabolism in alveolar macrophages (AMs). We performed whole-exome sequence analysis in a family affected by infantile-onset PAP with hypogammaglobulinemia without causative mutations in genes associated with PAP: SFTPB, SFTPC, ABCA3, CSF2RA, CSF2RB, and GATA2. We identified a heterozygous missense variation in OAS1, encoding 2, 0 5 0 -oligoadenylate synthetase 1 (OAS1) in three affected siblings, but not in unaffected family members. Deep sequence analysis with next-generation sequencing indicated 3.81% mosaicism of this variant in DNA from their mother's peripheral blood leukocytes, suggesting that PAP observed in this family could be inherited as an autosomal-dominant trait from the mother. We identified two additional de novo heterozygous missense variations of OAS1 in two unrelated simplex individuals also manifesting infantile-onset PAP with hypogammaglobulinemia. PAP in the two simplex individuals resolved after hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, indicating that OAS1 dysfunction is associated with impaired surfactant catabolism due to the defects in AMs.
instances.output: Heterozygous Mutations in OAS1 Cause Infantile-Onset Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis with Hypogammaglobulinemia

Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is a condition marked by the buildup of a surfactant-like substance in the alveoli, leading to respiratory failure due to low oxygen levels. Genetic pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (GPAP) arises from mutations in genes related to surfactant proteins or transporters in alveolar type II cells, as well as genes involved in surfactant breakdown in alveolar macrophages (AMs). 

In a study of a family with infantile-onset PAP and hypogammaglobulinemia, whole-exome sequencing revealed no mutations in known PAP-related genes (SFTPB, SFTPC, ABCA3, CSF2RA, CSF2RB, GATA2). However, a heterozygous missense mutation in the OAS1 gene, which encodes 2',5'-oligoadenylate synthetase 1, was found in three affected siblings but not in unaffected members. Further analysis showed 3.81% mosaicism of this variant in the mother's blood, suggesting an autosomal-dominant inheritance pattern. 

Additionally, two unrelated individuals with infantile-onset PAP and hypogammaglobulinemia were found to have de novo heterozygous missense mutations in OAS1. Their PAP symptoms improved following hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, supporting the idea that OAS1 dysfunction leads to issues with surfactant breakdown due to AM defects.
instances.mapper_output: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is characterized by the accumulation of surfactant-like substances in the alveoli, leading to respiratory failure. This study identifies a heterozygous missense variation in the OAS1 gene in a family with infantile-onset PAP and hypogammaglobulinemia, suggesting an autosomal-dominant inheritance pattern. Additionally, two unrelated individuals with similar symptoms also exhibited de novo OAS1 mutations, and their condition improved following hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. These findings indicate that OAS1 dysfunction may impair surfactant catabolism in alveolar macrophages, contributing to the pathogenesis of PAP.
instances.fs_output: Heterozygous Mutations in OAS1 Cause Infantile-Onset Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis with Hypogammaglobulinemia
instances.control_output:
Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is a condition marked by the buildup of a surfactant-like substance in the alveoli, leading to hypoxemic respiratory failure. Genetic pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (GPAP) arises from mutations in genes related to surfactant proteins or those involved in surfactant transport in alveolar type II epithelial cells. Additionally, mutations affecting surfactant breakdown in alveolar macrophages (AMs) can also cause GPAP. 

In a study involving a family with infantile-onset PAP and hypogammaglobulinemia, whole-exome sequencing was performed, revealing no mutations in known PAP-related genes such as SFTPB, SFTPC, ABCA3, CSF2RA, CSF2RB, and GATA2. However, a heterozygous missense mutation in the OAS1 gene, which encodes 2',5'-oligoadenylate synthetase 1, was found in three affected siblings but not in unaffected family members. Further analysis using next-generation sequencing showed that this variant was present at 3.81% mosaicism in the mother's peripheral blood leukocytes, suggesting an autosomal-dominant inheritance pattern from the mother.

Moreover, two unrelated simplex cases of infantile-onset PAP with hypogammaglobulinemia were identified, each carrying de novo heterozygous missense mutations in OAS1. Notably, the PAP in these simplex individuals resolved following hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, supporting the notion that OAS1 dysfunction leads to impaired surfactant catabolism due to defects in AMs.

instances.input: Newcastle disease (ND) is a contagious disease that affects most species of birds. Its causative pathogen, Newcastle disease virus (NDV), also exhibits considerable oncolytic activity against mammalian cancers. A better understanding of the pathogenesis of NDV will help us design efficient vaccines and novel anticancer strategies. GW3965, a widely used synthetic ligand of liver X receptor (LXR), induces the expression of LXRs and its downstream genes, including ATP-binding cassette transporter A1 (ABCA1). ABCA1 regulates cellular cholesterol homeostasis. Here, we found that GW3965 inhibited NDV infection in DF-1 cells. It also inhibited NF-jB activation and reduced the upregulation of proinflammatory cytokines induced by the infection. Further studies showed that GW3965 exerted its inhibitory effects on virus entry and replication. NDV infection increased the mRNA levels of several lipogenic genes but decreased the ABCA1 mRNA level. Overexpression of ABCA1 inhibited NDV infection and reduced the cholesterol content in DF-1 cells, but when the cholesterol was replenished, NDV infection was restored. GW3965 treatment prevented cholesterol accumulation in the perinuclear area of the infected cells. In summary, our studies suggest that GW3965 inhibits NDV infection, probably by affecting cholesterol homeostasis.
instances.output: The LXR ligand GW3965 inhibits Newcastle disease virus infection by affecting cholesterol homeostasis

instances.inspect_output: Newcastle disease (ND) is a contagious disease impacting various bird species, caused by the Newcastle disease virus (NDV), which also shows potential in treating mammalian cancers. Understanding NDV's pathogenesis is crucial for developing effective vaccines and cancer therapies. GW3965, a synthetic liver X receptor (LXR) ligand, enhances LXR expression and its target genes, including ATP-binding cassette transporter A1 (ABCA1), which is vital for cholesterol regulation. Our research indicates that GW3965 inhibits NDV infection in DF-1 cells by blocking virus entry and replication, while also suppressing NF-kB activation and proinflammatory cytokine production. Overexpressing ABCA1 reduces NDV infection and cholesterol levels in DF-1 cells, but restoring cholesterol reverses the infection. GW3965 treatment also prevents cholesterol accumulation around the nucleus in infected cells. In conclusion, GW3965 appears to inhibit NDV infection by modulating cholesterol homeostasis.

The findings reveal that GW3965 not only inhibits NDV infection and reduces proinflammatory cytokine levels but also impacts cholesterol homeostasis by regulating ABCA1 expression. Overall, the research suggests that GW3965 may serve as a therapeutic agent against NDV by modulating cholesterol levels within infected cells.

instances.input: Seasonal hyperacute panuveitis (SHAPU) is a potentially blinding ocular disease occurring in Nepal that principally affects young children. Random amplification of partially purified vitreous fluid (VF)-derived nucleic acid revealed the presence of human anelloviruses in VF of SHAPU patients. In a comparative study of patients with different ocular pathologies, SHAPU patients were at highest risk of harboring anelloviruses in their eyes. The majority of SHAPU patients had multiple anelloviruses in their VF. The ocular anellovirus load in SHAPU and non-SHAPU patients did not differ and no SHAPU-specific anellovirus variant was detected. Analysis of paired serum and VF samples from SHAPU and non-SHAPU patients showed that the anellovirus detected in VF samples most likely originated from the systemic viral pool during viremia, potentially through breakdown of the blood-ocular barrier. The detection of anelloviruses in VF samples of uveitis patients, profoundly so in SHAPU patients, is imperative and warrants elucidation of its clinical significance.
instances.output: High Prevalence of Anelloviruses in Vitreous Fluid of Children With Seasonal Hyperacute Panuveitis

instances.inspect_output: Seasonal hyperacute panuveitis (SHAPU) is a serious eye condition primarily affecting young children in Nepal. Research has shown that human anelloviruses are present in the vitreous fluid (VF) of SHAPU patients, with these patients having a higher likelihood of harboring multiple anelloviruses compared to those with other ocular conditions. Interestingly, the overall viral load of anelloviruses in SHAPU patients was similar to that in non-SHAPU patients, and no unique anellovirus variant specific to SHAPU was found. Further analysis of paired serum and VF samples indicated that the anelloviruses in the VF likely originated from the bloodstream during viremia, possibly due to a compromise in the blood-ocular barrier. The presence of anelloviruses in the VF of uveitis patients, especially in SHAPU cases, highlights the need for further investigation into the clinical implications of these findings.

instances.input: Background: Infectious disease surveillance is a process the product of which reflects both actual disease trends and public awareness of the disease. Decisions made by patients, health care providers, and public health professionals about seeking and providing health care and about reporting cases to health authorities are all influenced by the information environment, which changes constantly. Biases are therefore imbedded in surveillance systems; these biases need to be characterized to provide better situational awareness for decision-making purposes. Our goal is to develop a statistical framework to characterize influenza surveillance systems, particularly their correlation with the information environment. Methods: We identified Hong Kong influenza surveillance data systems covering healthcare providers, laboratories, daycare centers and residential care homes for the elderly. A Bayesian hierarchical statistical model was developed to examine the statistical relationships between the influenza surveillance data and the information environment represented by alerts from HealthMap and web queries from Google. Different models were fitted for non-pandemic and pandemic periods and model goodness-of-fit was assessed using common model selection procedures. Results: Some surveillance systemsespecially ad hoc systems developed in response to the pandemic flu outbreakare more correlated with the information environment than others. General practitioner (percentage of influenza-like-illness related patient visits among all patient visits) and laboratory (percentage of specimen tested positive) seem to proportionally reflect the actual disease trends and are less representative of the information environment. Surveillance systems using influenza-specific code for reporting tend to reflect biases of both healthcare seekers and providers. Conclusions: This study shows certain influenza surveillance systems are less correlated with the information environment than others, and therefore, might represent more reliable indicators of disease activity in future outbreaks. Although the patterns identified in this study might change in future outbreaks, the potential susceptibility of surveillance data is likely to persist in the future, and should be considered when interpreting surveillance data.
instances.output: Characterizing Influenza surveillance systems performance: application of a Bayesian hierarchical statistical model to Hong Kong surveillance data

instances.inspect_output:
**Title:** Characterizing Influenza Surveillance Systems in Relation to the Information Environment

**Background:** Infectious disease surveillance is influenced by both actual disease trends and public awareness. The decisions made by patients, healthcare providers, and public health professionals regarding healthcare-seeking behavior and case reporting are shaped by the constantly changing information environment. This leads to inherent biases in surveillance systems, which must be characterized to enhance situational awareness for decision-making.

**Objective:** To develop a statistical framework that characterizes influenza surveillance systems, focusing on their correlation with the information environment.

**Methods:** We analyzed influenza surveillance data from Hong Kong, which included data from healthcare providers, laboratories, daycare centers, and residential care homes for the elderly. A Bayesian hierarchical statistical model was created to explore the relationships between influenza surveillance data and the information environment, represented by alerts from HealthMap and Google web queries. Separate models were fitted for non-pandemic and pandemic periods, with model goodness-of-fit evaluated using standard selection procedures.

**Results:** The analysis revealed that certain surveillance systems, particularly those created in response to the pandemic flu outbreak, exhibited a stronger correlation with the information environment. In contrast, general practitioner data (percentage of influenza-like illness visits) and laboratory data (percentage of positive specimens) were found to more accurately reflect actual disease trends and were less influenced by the information environment. Surveillance systems that utilized influenza-specific reporting codes tended to reflect biases from both healthcare seekers and providers.

**Conclusions:** The study indicates that some influenza surveillance systems are more reliable indicators of disease activity than others, as they are less correlated with the information environment. While the patterns observed may evolve in future outbreaks, the susceptibility of surveillance data to biases is likely to remain a concern and should be taken into account when interpreting surveillance data.

Title: Characterizing Influenza Surveillance Systems in Relation to the Information Environment

This study investigates the biases inherent in influenza surveillance systems and their correlation with the information environment, which influences health-seeking behavior and reporting practices. By employing a Bayesian hierarchical statistical model on data from various surveillance sources in Hong Kong, the research identifies that certain systems, particularly those developed in response to pandemic outbreaks, show stronger correlations with public information trends. In contrast, traditional indicators like general practitioner visits and laboratory test positivity rates are found to more accurately reflect actual disease trends. The findings suggest that understanding these biases is crucial for improving the reliability of surveillance data in future influenza outbreaks.
instances.fs_output: Characterizing Biases in Influenza Surveillance Systems Relative to the Information Environment
instances.control_output: The study investigates the relationship between influenza surveillance systems and the information environment, highlighting the biases inherent in these systems. By employing a Bayesian hierarchical statistical model, the research analyzes data from various sources in Hong Kong, including healthcare providers and laboratories, during both pandemic and non-pandemic periods. The findings reveal that certain surveillance systems, particularly those developed in response to the pandemic, show a stronger correlation with public awareness and information sources, such as HealthMap alerts and Google web queries. In contrast, general practitioner visits and laboratory test results appear to more accurately reflect actual disease trends, making them potentially more reliable indicators of influenza activity. The study emphasizes the need to understand these biases in surveillance data to improve decision-making in public health, especially in future outbreaks.

instances.input: RNase L is an ankyrin repeat domain-containing dual endoribonuclease-pseudokinase that is activated by unusual 2, 0 5 0 -oligoadenylate (2-5A) second messengers and which impedes viral infections in higher vertebrates. Despite its importance in interferonregulated antiviral innate immunity, relatively little is known about its precise mechanism of action. Here we present a functional characterization of 2.5 Å and 3.25 Å X-ray crystal and small-angle X-ray scattering structures of RNase L bound to a natural 2-5A activator with and without ADP or the nonhydrolysable ATP mimetic AMP-PNP. These studies reveal how recognition of 2-5A through interactions with the ankyrin repeat domain and the pseudokinase domain, together with nucleotide binding, imposes a rigid intertwined dimer configuration that is essential for RNase catalytic and antiviral functions. The involvement of the pseudokinase domain of RNase L in 2-5A sensing, nucleotide binding, dimerization, and ribonuclease functions highlights the evolutionary adaptability of the eukaryotic protein kinase fold.
instances.output: Article Dimeric Structure of Pseudokinase RNase L Bound to 2-5A Reveals a Basis for Interferon-Induced Antiviral Activity

instances.inspect_output: RNase L is a dual endoribonuclease-pseudokinase that plays a crucial role in antiviral immunity by being activated by 2-5A second messengers. Despite its significance, the exact mechanism of RNase L's action is not well understood. Recent studies using X-ray crystallography and small-angle X-ray scattering have provided insights into its structure when bound to a natural 2-5A activator, with and without additional nucleotides like ADP or AMP-PNP. These findings demonstrate that the interaction of 2-5A with both the ankyrin repeat domain and the pseudokinase domain, along with nucleotide binding, leads to a stable dimer configuration necessary for RNase L's catalytic and antiviral activities. The role of the pseudokinase domain in sensing 2-5A, binding nucleotides, and facilitating dimerization underscores the evolutionary flexibility of the eukaryotic protein kinase structure.

The findings demonstrate that the binding of 2-5A induces a stable dimer configuration crucial for RNase L's catalytic and antiviral activities, emphasizing the role of the pseudokinase domain in these processes. Overall, this research enhances our understanding of RNase L's mechanism in antiviral immunity and its evolutionary significance.

instances.input: Background-Respiratory infections are the most common infectious diseases in humans worldwide and are a leading cause of death in children less than 5 years of age. Objectives-Identify candidate pathogens in pediatric patients with unexplained respiratory disease. Study Design-Forty-four nasopharyngeal washes collected during the 2004-05 winter season from pediatric patients with respiratory illnesses that tested negative for 7 common respiratory pathogens by culture and direct immunofluorescence assays were analyzed by MassTag-PCR. To distinguish human enteroviruses (HEV) and rhinoviruses (HRV), samples positive for picornaviruses were further characterized by sequence analysis. Results-Candidate pathogens were detected by MassTag PCR in 27 of the 44 (61%) specimens that previously were rated negative. Sixteen of these 27 specimens (59%) contained picornaviruses; of these 9 (57%) contained RNA of a recently discovered clade of rhinoviruses. Bocaviruses were detected in three patients by RT-PCR. Conclusions-Our study confirms that multiplex MassTag-PCR enhances the detection of pathogens in clinical specimens, and shows that previously unrecognized rhinoviruses, that potentially form a species HRV-C, may cause a significant amount of pediatric respiratory disease.
instances.output: Multiplex MassTag-PCR for Respiratory Pathogens in Pediatric Nasopharyngeal Washes Negative by Conventional Diagnostic Testing Shows a High Prevalence of Viruses Belonging to a Newly Recognized Picornavirus Clade

instances.inspect_output:
**Background:** Respiratory infections are the most prevalent infectious diseases globally and are a major cause of mortality in children under 5 years old.

**Objectives:** The aim of this study was to identify potential pathogens in pediatric patients suffering from unexplained respiratory diseases.

**Study Design:** The study analyzed 44 nasopharyngeal wash samples collected during the winter season of 2004-05 from pediatric patients with respiratory illnesses. These samples had previously tested negative for seven common respiratory pathogens using culture and direct immunofluorescence assays. The analysis was conducted using MassTag-PCR, and samples that tested positive for picornaviruses were further characterized through sequence analysis to differentiate between human enteroviruses (HEV) and rhinoviruses (HRV).

**Results:** Candidate pathogens were identified in 27 out of the 44 specimens (61%) that were initially negative. Among these, 16 specimens (59%) contained picornaviruses, with 9 of these (57%) showing RNA from a newly identified clade of rhinoviruses. Additionally, bocaviruses were detected in three patients using RT-PCR.

**Conclusions:** The findings of this study demonstrate that multiplex MassTag-PCR significantly improves the detection of pathogens in clinical samples. It also highlights the presence of previously unrecognized rhinoviruses, potentially classified as species HRV-C, which may contribute to a considerable amount of respiratory disease in pediatric patients.
instances.mapper_output:
Title: Identification of Candidate Pathogens in Pediatric Patients with Unexplained Respiratory Disease

Summary: This study investigates the presence of candidate pathogens in pediatric patients suffering from unexplained respiratory illnesses. Utilizing MassTag-PCR on nasopharyngeal washes from 44 children, the research identified pathogens in 61% of specimens that had previously tested negative for common respiratory viruses. Notably, a significant proportion of these contained recently discovered clades of rhinoviruses, suggesting their potential role in pediatric respiratory diseases. The findings highlight the effectiveness of multiplex MassTag-PCR in enhancing pathogen detection in clinical settings.
instances.fs_output: Multiplex MassTag-PCR Enhances Detection of Unrecognized Rhinoviruses in Pediatric Respiratory Illnesses
instances.control_output: The study highlights the prevalence of respiratory infections in pediatric patients and the challenges in identifying pathogens responsible for these illnesses. By utilizing MassTag-PCR, researchers were able to detect candidate pathogens in 61% of nasopharyngeal wash specimens that had previously tested negative for common respiratory viruses. Notably, a significant portion of these specimens contained picornaviruses, including a newly identified clade of rhinoviruses, which may contribute to pediatric respiratory diseases. Additionally, bocaviruses were identified in some patients. The findings underscore the effectiveness of advanced molecular techniques in enhancing pathogen detection and the potential role of unrecognized rhinoviruses in respiratory illnesses among children.

**Objectives:** This study aims to report an in-flight transmission cluster of COVID-19, as there was previously no data available on this topic, and to describe the clinical characteristics of the affected patients.

**Methods:** Following a flight, laboratory-confirmed COVID-19 cases were identified in 12 patients. Out of these, 10 patients were admitted to a designated hospital. Data collection occurred from January 25 to February 28, 2020, with clinical information gathered retrospectively.

**Results:** All patients were passengers, with no flight attendants affected. The median age of the patients was 33 years, and 70% were female. None of the patients required admission to the intensive care unit, and there were no fatalities reported by February 28. The median incubation period for the virus was 3.0 days, and the time from illness onset to hospital admission was 2 days. The most prevalent symptom among the patients was fever. Notably, two patients were asymptomatic and showed negative results on chest CT scans throughout their illness. Upon admission, initial RT-PCR tests were positive for 9 patients, while only half of the patients had positive initial chest CT results. The median lung "total severity score" on chest CT was 6, with notable findings including "Crazy-Paving" patterns, pleural effusion, and ground-glass nodules. 

All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission from the author/funder, who has granted medRxiv a license to display the preprint in perpetuity.
instances.mapper_output: **Objectives: No data were available about in-flight transmission of SARS-CoV-2. Here, we report an in-flight transmission cluster of COVID-19 and describe the clinical characteristics of these patients.** This study investigates a cluster of COVID-19 cases linked to a flight, involving 12 laboratory-confirmed patients, with a median age of 33 years and a predominance of females. The findings reveal that while the majority exhibited symptoms such as fever, two patients remained asymptomatic, and none required intensive care or experienced fatalities. Notably, the study highlights the variability in initial diagnostic results, with positive RT-PCR tests in most patients but only half showing abnormalities on chest CT scans.
instances.fs_output: In-Flight Transmission of SARS-CoV-2: Clinical Characteristics of a COVID-19 Cluster Among Passengers
instances.control_output:
The study reports an in-flight transmission cluster of COVID-19 involving 12 laboratory-confirmed cases. Key findings include:

- **Patient Demographics**: All patients were passengers, with a median age of 33 years; 70% were female.
- **Clinical Outcomes**: None required intensive care, and there were no fatalities reported by February 28, 2020.
- **Incubation and Admission**: The median incubation period was 3 days, and the time from illness onset to hospital admission was 2 days.
- **Symptoms**: The most prevalent symptom was fever, with two patients remaining asymptomatic and showing negative chest CT scans throughout their illness.
- **Diagnostic Findings**: On admission, 9 patients tested positive for COVID-19 via RT-PCR, while only half had positive chest CT findings. The median lung "total severity score" on chest CT was 6, with notable findings including "Crazy-Paving" patterns, pleural effusion, and ground-glass nodules.

This study highlights the potential for in-flight transmission of SARS-CoV-2 and provides insights into the clinical characteristics of affected patients.

instances.input: A B S T R A C T Interferons (IFN) are key cytokines with multifaceted antiviral and cell-modulatory properties. Three distinct types of IFN are recognized (I-III) based on structural features, receptor usage, cellular source and biological activities. The action of IFNs is mediated by a complex, partially overlapping, transcriptional program initiated by the interaction with specific receptors. Genetic diversity, with polymorphisms and mutations, can modulate the extent of IFN responses and the susceptibility to infections. Almost all viruses developed mechanisms to subvert the IFN response, involving both IFN induction and effector mechanisms. Interactions between IFN types may occur, for both antiviral and cell-modulatory effects, in a complex interplay, involving both synergistic and antagonistic effects. Interferon-associated diseases, not related to virus infections may occur, some of them frequently observed in IFN-treated patients. On the whole, IFNs are pleiotropic biologic response modifiers, that, upon activation of thousands genes, induce a broad spectrum of activities, regulating cell cycle, differentiation, plasma membrane molecules, release of mediators, etc., that can be relevant for cell proliferation, innate and adaptive immunity, hematopoiesis, angiogenesis and other body functions. ß

instances.inspect_output:
**Abstract**  
Interferons (IFN) are key cytokines with multifaceted antiviral and cell-modulatory properties. Three distinct types of IFN are recognized (I-III) based on structural features, receptor usage, cellular source, and biological activities. The action of IFNs is mediated by a complex, partially overlapping transcriptional program initiated by the interaction with specific receptors. Genetic diversity, with polymorphisms and mutations, can modulate the extent of IFN responses and the susceptibility to infections. Almost all viruses have developed mechanisms to subvert the IFN response, involving both IFN induction and effector mechanisms. Interactions between IFN types may occur, for both antiviral and cell-modulatory effects, in a complex interplay involving both synergistic and antagonistic effects. Interferon-associated diseases, not related to virus infections, may occur, some of which are frequently observed in IFN-treated patients. Overall, IFNs are pleiotropic biologic response modifiers that, upon activation of thousands of genes, induce a broad spectrum of activities, regulating cell cycle, differentiation, plasma membrane molecules, release of mediators, etc., which can be relevant for cell proliferation, innate and adaptive immunity, hematopoiesis, angiogenesis, and other body functions.
instances.mapper_output: A B S T R A C T Interferons (IFN) are crucial cytokines known for their diverse antiviral and cell-modulatory functions, categorized into three types (I-III) based on various characteristics. Their effects are mediated through a complex transcriptional program initiated by receptor interactions, with genetic variations influencing individual responses to IFNs and susceptibility to infections. Viruses have evolved mechanisms to evade IFN responses, highlighting the intricate interplay between different IFN types, which can exhibit both synergistic and antagonistic effects. Additionally, IFN-related diseases can arise in patients undergoing treatment, underscoring the broad biological impact of IFNs on various physiological processes, including immunity and cell regulation.
instances.fs_output: Interferons: Multifaceted Cytokines Modulating Antiviral Responses and Cellular Functions
instances.control_output: Interferons (IFN) are crucial cytokines with diverse antiviral and cell-modulatory functions. They are categorized into three types (I-III) based on their structural characteristics, receptor interactions, cellular origins, and biological roles. The effects of IFNs are mediated through a complex transcriptional program that is initiated by their binding to specific receptors. Genetic variations, including polymorphisms and mutations, can influence the magnitude of IFN responses and the vulnerability to infections. Most viruses have evolved strategies to evade the IFN response, affecting both the induction of IFN and its effector functions. There can be interactions among different IFN types that result in both synergistic and antagonistic effects, contributing to their antiviral and cell-modulatory roles. Additionally, interferon-associated diseases, which are not linked to viral infections, can arise, particularly in patients receiving IFN treatment. Overall, IFNs act as pleiotropic biological response modifiers, activating thousands of genes and leading to a wide range of activities that regulate cell cycle, differentiation, plasma membrane components, mediator release, and other processes relevant to cell proliferation, innate and adaptive immunity, hematopoiesis, angiogenesis, and various bodily functions.
instances.soft2_output: 1. Interferons: From Basic Research to Clinical Practice

instances.input: Several challenges remained to fabricate a molecular-level nucleic acid biosensor such as surface immobilization control, single mismatch detection and low current response. To overcome those issues, for the first time, authors presented a novel parallel structural dsDNA/recombinant azurin (PSD/rAzu) hybrid structure for the general nucleic acid detection. The PSD was designed and introduced by the optimized 8 Ag + ions to have greater conductivity than the canonical dsDNA, and conjugated with rAzu to develop a general platform for electrochemical detection of miRNAs and viral DNAs with high reproducibility and ultra-sensitivity towards single base pair mutation. Thanks to the bifunctional rAzu as the selective spacer and electrochemical signal mediator, in the presence of the target strand, the imperfect PSD switched rapidly to the upright position where the Ag + ions intercalated between C-C mismatches of dsDNAs at the top of each structure brought further from the electrode surface resulting in a significant electrochemical signal drop of the Ag + ions. The charge transfer (CT) mechanism across the hybrid structure was simply clarified on the basis of the redox potential location of the species. The electrical conductivity of DNAs were measured using scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS) at the molecular scale and cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique based on the reduction of Ag + ion. The proposed PSD/rAzu hybrid structure with a great capability of single mutation recognition and miRNA expression level profiling in cancer cells holds a very promising platform to be studied for further development of various kinds of nanoscale biosensors, bioelectronic devices.
instances.output: Electrochemical nucleic acid detection based on parallel structural dsDNA/recombinant azurin hybrid

instances.inspect_output:
The development of a molecular-level nucleic acid biosensor faces several challenges, including control over surface immobilization, detection of single mismatches, and achieving low current responses. To address these issues, the authors introduced a novel parallel structural dsDNA/recombinant azurin (PSD/rAzu) hybrid structure aimed at enhancing nucleic acid detection. The PSD was engineered with optimized 8 Ag+ ions to improve conductivity compared to traditional dsDNA, and it was conjugated with rAzu to create a versatile platform for the electrochemical detection of miRNAs and viral DNAs, demonstrating high reproducibility and ultra-sensitivity to single base pair mutations.

The bifunctional rAzu acts as both a selective spacer and an electrochemical signal mediator. When the target strand is present, the imperfect PSD rapidly transitions to an upright position, allowing Ag+ ions to intercalate between C-C mismatches in the dsDNAs. This movement positions the mismatches further from the electrode surface, leading to a significant drop in the electrochemical signal of the Ag+ ions. The charge transfer (CT) mechanism across the hybrid structure is explained by the redox potential location of the involved species.

The electrical conductivity of the DNAs was assessed using scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS) at the molecular scale and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques, focusing on the reduction of Ag+ ions. The proposed PSD/rAzu hybrid structure shows great potential for recognizing single mutations and profiling miRNA expression levels in cancer cells, making it a promising platform for the future development of various nanoscale biosensors and bioelectronic devices.
instances.mapper_output: Several challenges remained to fabricate a molecular-level nucleic acid biosensor such as surface immobilization control, single mismatch detection and low current response. This study introduces a novel parallel structural dsDNA/recombinant azurin (PSD/rAzu) hybrid structure that enhances electrochemical detection of miRNAs and viral DNAs, achieving high sensitivity and reproducibility for single base pair mutations. The innovative design incorporates optimized Ag + ions to improve conductivity, while the bifunctional rAzu acts as a selective spacer and signal mediator, facilitating a significant electrochemical response upon target strand binding. The findings suggest that the PSD/rAzu hybrid structure has substantial potential for advancing nanoscale biosensors and bioelectronic devices, particularly in cancer diagnostics.
instances.fs_output: A Novel Parallel Structural dsDNA/Recombinant Azurin Hybrid for Ultra-Sensitive Nucleic Acid Detection and Single Mutation Recognition

instances.input: Graphical Abstract Highlights d TRIM25 binds to influenza ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs) in the nucleus d On vRNPs, TRIM25 acts as a clamp to inhibit viral RNA chain elongation d TRIM25 restriction of influenza RNA synthesis can be reconstituted in vitro d Influenza NS1 protein antagonizes this anti-influenza activity of TRIM25 SUMMARY TRIM25 is an E3 ubiquitin ligase that activates RIG-I to promote the antiviral interferon response. The NS1 protein from all strains of influenza A virus binds TRIM25, although not all virus strains block the interferon response, suggesting alternative mechanisms for TRIM25 action. Here we present a nuclear role for TRIM25 in specifically restricting influenza A virus replication. TRIM25 inhibits viral RNA synthesis through a direct mechanism that is independent of its ubiquitin ligase activity and the interferon pathway. This activity can be inhibited by the viral NS1 protein. TRIM25 inhibition of viral RNA synthesis results from its binding to viral ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs), the structures containing individual viral RNA segments, the viral polymerase, and multiple viral nucleoproteins. TRIM25 binding does not inhibit initiation of capped-RNA-primed viral mRNA synthesis by the viral polymerase. Rather, the onset of RNA chain elongation is inhibited because TRIM25 prohibits the movement of RNA into the polymerase complex.

The NS1 protein from all strains of influenza A virus binds TRIM25, although not all virus strains block the interferon response, suggesting alternative mechanisms for TRIM25 action. Here we present a nuclear role for TRIM25 in specifically restricting influenza A virus replication. TRIM25 inhibits viral RNA synthesis through a direct mechanism that is independent of its ubiquitin ligase activity and the interferon pathway. This activity can be inhibited by the viral NS1 protein. TRIM25 inhibition of viral RNA synthesis results from its binding to viral ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs), the structures containing individual viral RNA segments, the viral polymerase, and multiple viral nucleoproteins. TRIM25 binding does not inhibit initiation of capped-RNA-primed viral mRNA synthesis by the viral polymerase. Rather, the onset of RNA chain elongation is inhibited because TRIM25 prohibits the movement of RNA into the polymerase complex. The TRIM25-viral RNA interaction is specific and can be reconstituted in vitro, demonstrating that TRIM25 is a potent inhibitor of influenza A virus replication. The mechanism of TRIM25 action highlights the importance of the host cell nucleus as a site of antiviral activity. instances.inspect_output:
**Graphical Abstract Highlights:**
- TRIM25 binds to influenza ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs) in the nucleus.
- On vRNPs, TRIM25 acts as a clamp to inhibit viral RNA chain elongation.
- TRIM25 restriction of influenza RNA synthesis can be reconstituted in vitro.
- Influenza NS1 protein antagonizes this anti-influenza activity of TRIM25.

**Summary:**
TRIM25 is an E3 ubiquitin ligase that activates RIG-I to enhance the antiviral interferon response. The NS1 protein from all strains of influenza A virus interacts with TRIM25, although not all strains effectively block the interferon response, indicating that TRIM25 may operate through alternative mechanisms. This study reveals a nuclear function of TRIM25 in specifically limiting influenza A virus replication. TRIM25 inhibits viral RNA synthesis through a direct mechanism that does not rely on its ubiquitin ligase activity or the interferon pathway. The viral NS1 protein can inhibit this activity of TRIM25. TRIM25's inhibition of viral RNA synthesis occurs through its binding to viral ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs), which consist of viral RNA segments, the viral polymerase, and various viral nucleoproteins. Importantly, TRIM25 does not prevent the initiation of capped-RNA-primed viral mRNA synthesis by the polymerase; instead, it obstructs RNA chain elongation by restricting RNA movement within the polymerase complex.
instances.mapper_output:
**Graphical Abstract Highlights**  
d TRIM25 binds to influenza ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs) in the nucleus  
d On vRNPs, TRIM25 acts as a clamp to inhibit viral RNA chain elongation  
d TRIM25 restriction of influenza RNA synthesis can be reconstituted in vitro  
d Influenza NS1 protein antagonizes this anti-influenza activity of TRIM25  

**SUMMARY**  
This study reveals a novel role for TRIM25, an E3 ubiquitin ligase, in restricting influenza A virus replication by directly inhibiting viral RNA synthesis in the nucleus. TRIM25 binds to viral ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs) and acts as a clamp that prevents RNA chain elongation, independent of its ubiquitin ligase activity and the interferon response. The viral NS1 protein can counteract this restriction, highlighting a complex interaction between host antiviral mechanisms and viral evasion strategies.
instances.fs_output: TRIM25 Restricts Influenza A Virus Replication by Inhibiting Viral RNA Chain Elongation
instances.control_output: TRIM25, an E3 ubiquitin ligase, plays a crucial role in the antiviral response by activating RIG-I to promote interferon production. However, the influenza A virus's NS1 protein can bind to TRIM25, which complicates its function in different virus strains. This study reveals that TRIM25 has a nuclear function in restricting influenza A virus replication by directly inhibiting viral RNA synthesis, independent of its ubiquitin ligase activity and the interferon pathway. TRIM25 binds to viral ribonucleoproteins (vRNPs), which consist of viral RNA segments and proteins, and this binding does not prevent the initiation of viral mRNA synthesis. Instead, TRIM25 acts as a clamp that inhibits RNA chain elongation by blocking the movement of RNA into the polymerase complex, a process that can be antagonized by the NS1 protein of the virus.

instances.inspect_output: Some theories argue that civil disobedience is justified in regard to unjust laws or government actions that violate fundamental human rights or moral principles. Proponents of civil disobedience believe that when legal channels fail to address grievances or when laws are inherently unjust, individuals have a moral obligation to resist and challenge those laws through nonviolent means. This perspective is often rooted in the belief that justice and morality should take precedence over legal compliance, especially in cases where the law perpetuates inequality or oppression.

Loudoun's fall from power as military advisor to Newcastle can be attributed to several factors:

1. **Military Failures**: Loudoun's strategies and decisions during military campaigns, particularly in the French and Indian War, were criticized. His inability to achieve significant victories and the setbacks faced by British forces undermined his credibility.

2. **Political Rivalries**: The political landscape in Britain was tumultuous, and Loudoun faced opposition from other political figures and military leaders. Rivalries within the government and military often influenced decisions regarding leadership and appointments.

3. **Public and Parliamentary Pressure**: As military failures mounted, public opinion and parliamentary pressure grew against Loudoun. Calls for accountability and change in leadership became louder, leading to a loss of support for him.

4. **New Leadership**: The appointment of new military leaders who were seen as more capable or who had better relationships with the political establishment also contributed to Loudoun's decline. The emergence of figures like General Amherst shifted the focus away from Loudoun.

5. **Strategic Misalignment**: Loudoun's strategies may not have aligned with the broader objectives of the British government or military, leading to a disconnect that further eroded his position.

These factors combined to lead to Loudoun's eventual removal from his position as military advisor.

2. **Conflict with Colonial Leaders**: Loudoun often clashed with colonial governors and military leaders, which created friction and undermined his authority. His approach was sometimes seen as overly rigid and dismissive of local knowledge and strategies.

3. **Logistical Issues**: The logistical challenges of supplying and coordinating troops in the colonies were exacerbated under Loudoun's command. His strategies often failed to account for the unique conditions of warfare in North America.

4. **Political Pressure**: As the war progressed, political pressure mounted from both British officials and colonial leaders who were dissatisfied with the progress of the war. This pressure contributed to a loss of confidence in Loudoun's leadership.

5. **Change in Strategy**: The British government began to shift its military strategy, favoring more aggressive and flexible approaches that Loudoun was not aligned with. This shift made his leadership style seem outdated.

Ultimately, these factors combined to lead to Loudoun's removal from his position as military advisor, paving the way for new leadership that was more in tune with the evolving needs of the war effort.

 Reason: The 'him' refers to john because You can't hire someone to take care of themselves. 
 Question: Is the above reasoning correct or wrong?
instances.inspect_output: The reasoning is correct. In the sentence, "John hired Bill to take care of him," the pronoun "him" indeed refers to John, as it is logical that John would hire someone (Bill) to take care of him (John) rather than hiring someone to take care of themselves.
instances.control_output: The reasoning is correct. The pronoun 'him' in the sentence refers to John, as it is logical to hire someone to take care of another person, not oneself.

instances.input:
Sentence: When the sponsors of the bill got to the town hall, they were surprised to find that the room was full of opponents. They were very much in the minority.
 Reason: The 'They' refers to the sponsors because the bill does not have the support of the majority of the city council. 
 Question: Is the above reasoning correct or wrong?
instances.soft_output: Wrong. The 'They' refers to the sponsors of the bill, not the bill itself.
instances.inspect_output: The reasoning is wrong. The 'They' in the second sentence refers to the opponents, not the sponsors. The sentence states that the sponsors were surprised to find that the room was full of opponents, indicating that the opponents are the ones who are in the minority. The sponsors are not in the minority; rather, they are the ones who are proposing the bill.
instances.control_output: The reasoning is wrong. The 'They' in the second sentence refers to the opponents, not the sponsors. The sponsors are the ones who are surprised to find that the room is full of opponents, indicating that the opponents are in the majority, while the sponsors are in the minority.

instances.input:
Sentence: Frank felt vindicated when his longtime rival Bill revealed that he was the winner of the competition.

instances.inspect_output: The reasoning is wrong. In the sentence, "he" refers to Bill, not Frank. The context indicates that Bill is the one revealing the information about the competition, and Frank is the one feeling vindicated. Therefore, the pronoun "he" does not refer to Frank.
instances.control_output: The reasoning is wrong. The pronoun 'he' in the sentence refers to Bill, not Frank. The sentence states that Bill revealed he was the winner, which indicates that 'he' is Bill. Frank felt vindicated, but the pronoun does not refer to him.

instances.input:
Overall, not very impressed. This is the 2nd ASUS monitor I have, I have the VE228 as well in a dual monitor display. The colors on this monitor suck, plain and simple. Every time I sit down I think it looks too red, too washed out, too green after I adjust the red, the colors are not consistent at all, and the brightness suffers because of it. Meanwhile, the other ASUS monitor I have that's 3 years old is exactly what I wanted this one to be.

The monitor wobbles while I'm typing, it's not secured to the base very well. I can tap the sides of both monitors, there is much more "shake" on this new monitor than the older one.

It also seems to have a problem "waking up" when the computer has sat for a while. I have to turn the power off and back on to get it to recognize, while the old monitor (currently set as monitor #2) works fine.

I'm not very impressed, I am thinking of upgrading my ASUS motherboard to a newer one, but now have to exercise caution.

instances.inspect_output:
It sounds like you're having a frustrating experience with your new ASUS monitor. Here are some key points from your review:

1. **Color Issues**: You find the colors on the new monitor to be inconsistent, often appearing too red, washed out, or too green. This is disappointing compared to your older ASUS monitor, which meets your expectations.

2. **Stability Problems**: The new monitor wobbles while typing, indicating that it is not securely attached to its base. This is a significant concern, especially when compared to the stability of your older monitor.

3. **Wake-Up Problem**: The monitor has difficulty waking up after being idle, requiring you to power it off and on again. This issue does not occur with your older monitor.

4. **Overall Impression**: You are not impressed with the new monitor and are reconsidering your options for upgrading your ASUS motherboard due to this experience.

It seems like you have valid concerns about the performance and reliability of the new monitor. If you continue to experience these issues, it might be worth reaching out to ASUS support for assistance or considering a different model.

They compare it unfavorably to an older ASUS monitor, which meets their expectations. Additionally, the new monitor wobbles during use, indicating instability, and has trouble waking up from sleep mode, requiring a power cycle to function again. Overall, the reviewer is not satisfied and is reconsidering future ASUS purchases, including a potential motherboard upgrade.

instances.input: How is the life of a math student? Could you describe your own experiences?
instances.output: Which level of prepration is enough for the exam jlpt5?
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output:
The life of a math student can be both challenging and rewarding. It often involves a mix of rigorous coursework, problem-solving, and collaboration with peers. Here are some common experiences that many math students might encounter:

1. **Coursework**: Math students typically take a variety of courses, ranging from calculus and linear algebra to more advanced topics like abstract algebra and real analysis. Each course requires a strong understanding of concepts and the ability to apply them to solve problems.

2. **Problem-Solving**: A significant part of being a math student involves tackling complex problems. This can be both frustrating and exhilarating. Many students spend hours working through difficult problems, and the satisfaction of finally solving a tough question is a rewarding experience.

3. **Study Groups**: Collaboration is key in math. Many students form study groups to discuss concepts, share different approaches to problems, and help each other understand difficult material. This social aspect can make studying more enjoyable and effective.

4. **Office Hours**: Attending office hours is common for math students. Professors and teaching assistants are usually very willing to help students who seek clarification on topics or need assistance with homework.

5. **Projects and Research**: Depending on the level of study, math students may engage in projects or research. This can involve exploring a specific area of mathematics in depth, which can be a great way to apply what they've learned and delve into topics of personal interest.

6. **Exams and Assessments**: Math students often face a variety of assessments, including quizzes, midterms, and finals. Preparing for these can be intense, as they often require a deep understanding of the material and the ability to perform under pressure.

7. **Career Opportunities**: Many math students are motivated by the career opportunities that a strong math background can provide. Fields such as finance, data science, engineering, and academia are common paths for math graduates.

Overall, the life of a math student is filled with intellectual challenges, opportunities for collaboration, and the joy of discovery in the world of mathematics. Each student's experience may vary, but the common thread is a passion for problem-solving and a love for the subject.
instances.mapper_output: What has been your experience as a math student? My life as a math student has been challenging yet rewarding, filled with problem-solving and critical thinking.
instances.fs_output: What are the challenges faced by students studying mathematics at university?
instances.control_output:
The life of a math student can be both challenging and rewarding. It often involves a rigorous schedule filled with lectures, problem sets, and study sessions. Here are some common experiences that many math students might encounter:

1. **Intense Coursework**: Math students typically take a variety of courses, from calculus and linear algebra to more advanced topics like abstract algebra and real analysis. Each course requires a deep understanding of concepts and the ability to apply them to solve complex problems.

2. **Problem Solving**: A significant part of being a math student involves working through problem sets. This can be time-consuming and sometimes frustrating, but it also fosters critical thinking and analytical skills. Many students find joy in the challenge of solving difficult problems.

3. **Study Groups**: Collaboration is key in math. Many students form study groups to discuss concepts, work through problems together, and prepare for exams. This social aspect can make studying more enjoyable and less isolating.

4. **Office Hours and Tutoring**: Math students often take advantage of professors' office hours to seek help on difficult topics. Additionally, many students may work as tutors, helping others while reinforcing their own understanding.

5. **Research Opportunities**: For those interested in pursuing graduate studies, getting involved in research can be a significant part of a math student’s life. This can involve working on projects with professors or participating in summer research programs.

6. **Exams and Stress**: The pressure of exams can be intense, especially in upper-level courses. Many students experience stress during midterms and finals, but developing good study habits and time management skills can help alleviate some of this pressure.

7. **Community and Events**: Many math departments host events, seminars, and guest lectures, providing opportunities for students to engage with the broader mathematical community and learn about current research and applications.

8. **Career Paths**: Math students often explore various career paths, including academia, finance, data science, engineering, and more. Internships and networking can play a crucial role in shaping their future careers.

Overall, the life of a math student is filled with challenges that can lead to personal growth and a deep appreciation for the beauty of mathematics. The journey can be tough, but the skills and knowledge gained are invaluable.
instances.soft2_output: 1. What is the best way to learn math?

instances.input: What causes stool color to change to yellow?
instances.output: What can cause stool to come out as little balls?
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output:
Stool color can change to yellow for several reasons, including:

1. **Diet**: Consuming foods high in fat, such as fried foods or certain dairy products, can lead to yellow stool. Additionally, foods like carrots or sweet potatoes, which are high in beta-carotene, can also cause a yellow tint.

2. **Bile Production**: Bile is a digestive fluid produced by the liver that helps break down fats. If there is an issue with bile production or flow, such as in cases of liver disease or bile duct obstruction, stool may appear yellow.

3. **Malabsorption**: Conditions that affect the absorption of nutrients, such as celiac disease or pancreatitis, can lead to yellow, greasy, and foul-smelling stools.

4. **Infections**: Certain gastrointestinal infections, particularly those caused by bacteria or parasites, can alter stool color and consistency.

5. **Medications**: Some medications or supplements, particularly those containing iron or certain antibiotics, can change stool color.

If yellow stool persists or is accompanied by other symptoms, it is advisable to consult a healthcare professional for further evaluation.
instances.mapper_output: What causes stool color to change to yellow? A change in diet, certain medications, or malabsorption issues can lead to yellow stool.
instances.fs_output: What can cause stool to appear pale or clay-colored?
instances.control_output:
Stool color can change to yellow due to several factors, including:

1. **Diet**: Consuming foods high in fat, such as fried foods or certain dairy products, can lead to yellow stool. Additionally, foods with yellow pigments, like carrots or sweet potatoes, can also affect stool color.

2. **Malabsorption**: Conditions that affect the absorption of nutrients, such as celiac disease, pancreatitis, or cystic fibrosis, can result in yellow, greasy, and foul-smelling stools.

3. **Bile Production**: Yellow stool can indicate that bile is not being properly processed. Bile is a digestive fluid produced by the liver, and if it doesn't mix well with stool, it can lead to a yellow color.

4. **Infections**: Certain gastrointestinal infections, particularly those caused by bacteria or parasites, can lead to changes in stool color, including yellow.

5. **Medications**: Some medications or supplements, particularly those containing iron or certain antibiotics, can alter stool color.

If yellow stool persists or is accompanied by other symptoms such as diarrhea, abdominal pain, or weight loss, it is advisable to consult a healthcare professional for further evaluation.
instances.soft2_output: What causes stool color to change to green?

instances.input: How is air traffic controlled?
instances.output: How do you become an air traffic controller?
instances.inspect_output:
Air traffic is controlled through a system of regulations, procedures, and technologies designed to ensure the safe and efficient movement of aircraft in the airspace and on the ground. Here are the key components of air traffic control:

1. **Air Traffic Control Centers (ATCCs)**: These facilities manage air traffic in specific regions of airspace. They use radar and other technologies to track aircraft and provide instructions to pilots.

2. **Air Traffic Controllers (ATCs)**: Trained professionals who monitor and direct aircraft movements. They communicate with pilots via radio, providing instructions for takeoff, landing, and in-flight navigation.

3. **Flight Plans**: Before a flight, pilots file a flight plan that includes the intended route, altitude, and other details. This plan is reviewed by air traffic controllers to ensure safety and efficiency.

4. **Separation Standards**: Controllers maintain safe distances between aircraft to prevent collisions. This involves vertical separation (altitude differences) and horizontal separation (lateral distance).

5. **Radar and Surveillance Systems**: ATCs use radar systems to track aircraft positions in real-time. Modern systems may also include Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast (ADS-B), which allows aircraft to broadcast their position.

6. **Communication Systems**: Controllers and pilots communicate using standardized phraseology to ensure clarity and reduce misunderstandings. This includes the use of VHF radio frequencies.

7. **Airspace Classification**: Airspace is divided into different classes (A, B, C, D, E, and G) based on the level of control and the type of operations permitted. Each class has specific rules and requirements for aircraft.

8. **Ground Control**: At airports, ground controllers manage the movement of aircraft on the taxiways and runways, ensuring safe operations during takeoff, landing, and taxiing.

9. **Coordination with Other Agencies**: Air traffic control works in coordination with various agencies, including military, international, and airport authorities, to manage airspace effectively.

10. **Emergency Procedures**: Controllers are trained to handle emergencies, providing assistance to pilots in distress and coordinating with emergency services as needed.

Overall, air traffic control is a complex and critical system that relies on technology, communication, and human expertise to maintain safety in the skies.
instances.mapper_output: What system is used to manage the movement of aircraft in the sky and on the ground? Air traffic is controlled through a network of air traffic controllers and radar systems that ensure safe distances between aircraft.
instances.fs_output: What are the main methods used to manage and direct air traffic in busy airports?
instances.control_output:
Air traffic is controlled through a system of regulations, procedures, and technologies designed to ensure the safe and efficient movement of aircraft in the airspace and on the ground. Here are the key components of air traffic control:

1. **Air Traffic Control Centers (ATCCs)**: These facilities manage air traffic in specific regions of airspace. They use radar and communication systems to track aircraft and provide instructions to pilots.

2. **Air Traffic Controllers (ATCs)**: Trained professionals who monitor and direct aircraft movements. They provide clearances for takeoff and landing, manage flight paths, and ensure safe distances between aircraft.

3. **Communication**: Pilots and air traffic controllers communicate via radio. Controllers provide instructions regarding altitude, speed, and route changes, while pilots report their status and any issues.

4. **Flight Plans**: Before a flight, pilots file a flight plan that outlines the intended route, altitude, and other details. This plan is reviewed by air traffic control to ensure safety and efficiency.

5. **Radar and Surveillance Systems**: ATCs use radar systems to track aircraft positions in real-time. Advanced systems like Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast (ADS-B) provide more accurate tracking data.

6. **Separation Standards**: Controllers maintain safe distances between aircraft, both vertically and horizontally, to prevent collisions. These standards vary based on the type of airspace and aircraft.

7. **Airspace Classification**: Airspace is divided into different classes (A, B, C, D, E, and G) based on traffic density and the level of control required. Each class has specific rules and requirements for pilots and controllers.

8. **Procedures and Protocols**: Established procedures guide controllers in managing traffic, including standard approaches and departures, emergency protocols, and handling of special situations like weather disruptions.

9. **Technology and Automation**: Modern air traffic control relies on advanced technology, including automated systems that assist controllers in managing traffic and improving safety.

10. **Coordination with Other Agencies**: Air traffic control works closely with other aviation authorities, including airport operations, military air traffic control, and international agencies, to ensure seamless operations across borders.

Overall, air traffic control is a complex and critical system that ensures the safety and efficiency of air travel.

instances.input: Can I enter University of Melbourne if I couldn't achieve the guaranteed marks in Trinity College Foundation?
instances.output: University of the Philippines: If I take a second BFA in the UP College of Fine Arts, can I be exempted from gen. ed. or core subjects?
instances.inspect_output: If you did not achieve the guaranteed marks required for entry into the University of Melbourne from Trinity College Foundation Studies, you may still have options. The University of Melbourne may consider your application based on other factors, such as your overall academic performance, personal statement, or other qualifications. It is best to contact the admissions office of the University of Melbourne directly for specific guidance regarding your situation and to explore any alternative pathways for admission.
instances.mapper_output: What are my options for entering the University of Melbourne if I didn't meet the guaranteed marks in Trinity College Foundation? You may still be able to enter the University of Melbourne through alternative pathways or by meeting other admission requirements.
instances.fs_output: What are the alternative pathways to gain admission to the University of Melbourne if I don't meet the guaranteed marks in Trinity College Foundation?
instances.control_output:
If you did not achieve the guaranteed marks in the Trinity College Foundation Studies program, you may still have options to enter the University of Melbourne, but it will depend on several factors. You can consider the following:

1. **Alternative Entry Pathways**: The University of Melbourne may have alternative entry pathways for students who do not meet the guaranteed marks. This could include applying through other qualifications or programs.

2. **Application for Admission**: You can still apply for admission to the University of Melbourne, and your application will be considered based on your overall academic performance and other criteria.

3. **Special Consideration**: If there were extenuating circumstances that affected your performance, you might be able to apply for special consideration.

instances.input: What is the responsibility of SAP ERP key user?
instances.output: What is a qualified SAP ERP key user?
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output:
The responsibility of an SAP ERP key user includes the following:

1. **System Expertise**: Key users are expected to have in-depth knowledge of the SAP ERP system and its functionalities relevant to their specific business area.

2. **User Support**: They provide support and assistance to end-users, helping them navigate the system and resolve any issues that arise during their daily operations.

3. **Training**: Key users often conduct training sessions for new users and provide ongoing training for existing users to ensure they are proficient in using the system.

4. **Process Improvement**: They identify opportunities for process improvements within the ERP system and work on optimizing workflows to enhance efficiency.

5. **Testing and Validation**: Key users participate in testing new functionalities, updates, and enhancements to ensure they meet business requirements and function correctly.

6. **Communication Liaison**: They act as a bridge between the IT department and business users, communicating user needs and feedback to the IT team for system enhancements.

7. **Documentation**: Key users may be responsible for creating and maintaining documentation related to processes, user guides, and system changes.

8. **Change Management**: They help manage changes within the ERP system, ensuring that users are informed and prepared for any updates or new features.

Overall, key users play a crucial role in ensuring that the SAP ERP system is effectively utilized within the organization, facilitating smooth operations and user satisfaction.
instances.mapper_output: What is the responsibility of an SAP ERP key user? The key user is responsible for acting as a liaison between the end users and the IT department, ensuring that the system meets business needs and providing support and training to users.

6. **Documentation**: Creating and maintaining documentation related to processes, user guides, and training materials.

7. **Change Management**: Assisting in change management activities, helping users adapt to new processes or system changes.

8. **Issue Resolution**: Troubleshooting and resolving user issues or escalating them to the appropriate technical teams when necessary.

9. **Data Management**: Ensuring data integrity and accuracy within the SAP system by monitoring and managing data inputs and outputs.

10. **Continuous Learning**: Staying updated on SAP developments, new features, and best practices to enhance their knowledge and support capabilities. 

Overall, key users play a crucial role in ensuring the successful implementation and ongoing use of SAP ERP systems within an organization.

instances.input: How is being gay or lesbian less moral than divorce?
instances.output: Why do a lot of theists and agnostics confuse mainstream atheistic thought with "positive atheism"?
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output:
The morality of being gay or lesbian compared to divorce is a complex and subjective topic that often depends on individual beliefs, cultural backgrounds, and religious views. 

1. **Cultural and Religious Perspectives**: Some cultures and religions may view same-sex relationships as immoral based on traditional beliefs about marriage and sexuality. In contrast, divorce is often seen as a more acceptable option when a marriage is unhappy or unhealthy, even if it involves breaking vows.

2. **Nature of Relationships**: Supporters of LGBTQ+ rights argue that being gay or lesbian is a natural expression of love and identity, while divorce is often associated with failure or conflict in a relationship. This can lead to differing views on the morality of each situation.

3. **Social Acceptance**: In many societies, divorce has become more normalized and accepted, while same-sex relationships may still face stigma. This societal acceptance can influence perceptions of morality.

4. **Intent and Impact**: Some may argue that divorce can lead to emotional pain for families, while same-sex relationships are based on love and consent. Others may argue that both can have moral implications depending on the circumstances.

Ultimately, the question of morality is deeply personal and varies widely among individuals and communities.

instances.control_output:

In contrast, divorce is often viewed through the lens of commitment and vows made in marriage, which some may see as a moral failing if those vows are broken.

However, it's important to note that morality is not universally defined, and different societies and individuals have different standards. Some may view divorce as a necessary step for personal happiness and well-being, while others may see it as a negative outcome. Ultimately, the comparison between the two involves deeply held beliefs and values that can differ widely among people.

instances.input: The 12-day event drew to a close on Sunday after screening 134 new features. There were more than 50,000 admissions at venues across the city - an increase on last year's figure. The festival sold tickets for theatres including the EIFF's home at the Filmhouse. It also sold tickets at its venue partner Cineworld, and at the Festival Theatre Edinburgh, the Traverse, Dominion Cinema, Odeon Lothian Road, Royal Lyceum Theatre and Belmont Filmhouse in Aberdeen. Organisers said an additional 14,600 people enjoyed two weekends of outdoor screenings as part of the Film in the City event. Opening night Actor Robert Carlyle's directorial debut The Legend of Barney Thomson opened the 2015 festival - in its 69th year - where it received its world premiere ahead of its release at the end of July. Ken Hay, EIFF chief executive said there had been some "truly memorable moments", including the opening night and Ewan McGregor's sold-out in-person event at the Lyceum. "Our audiences have obviously responded to our rich and diverse programme, and we are thrilled to see the increase in admissions," he said. As the event closed, it was announced Scottish actors James Cosmo and Karen Gillan had been made EIFF honorary patrons. Cosmo has appeared in films such as Highlander and Braveheart while Gillan is known for her role as Amy Pond in Doctor Who.

instances.input: Karl Turner, Emma Hardy and Diana Johnson fear thousands of jobs are at risk because of the pandemic disrupting much of the seasonal trade. According to the National Caravan Council, East Yorkshire is at the "centre of caravan manufacturing". The Treasury said there was a wide range of schemes for different sectors. Mr Turner, MP for Hull East, said the 20,000 jobs were "either directly in or related to" production and sales. "As a seasonal business, there is a very real threat that if support is not continued, we will see job losses later in the year," he said. "We must not see a repeat of what happened to the industry locally after the 2008 crash." In a letter to Chancellor Rishi Sunak, the MPs said the industry contributed £9bn a year to the UK economy and that it was at "serious risk of collapse without further support for the sector". "A flexible, sector-focused approach to ending the furlough scheme, that allows manufacturers to respond to market conditions, would allow the industry to come through this crisis," the MPs said in the letter, adding that "livelihoods depend on the survival of this industry". A Treasury spokesperson said it would "continue to support businesses as the economy reopens and people return to work" with measures including business rates holidays and cash grants of up to £25,000 for eligible firms. Follow BBC East Yorkshire and Lincolnshire on Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram. Send your story ideas to yorkslincs.news@bbc.co.uk.
