instances.input: The Health Hydro in Milton Road, Swindon, has been upgraded from Grade II listed status by Historic England. Operator Greenwich Leisure Limited had said it was considering converting it into residential space but a petition urging the firm not to close or alter the baths attracted 7,000 signatures. The upgraded status would mean Historic England would have to be consulted about any renovations or developments. Historic England said it upgraded the status of the building, which is owned by the local authority, in recognition of its historic and architectural interest. The hydro was designed to provide health and welfare services for workers at Brunel's Great Western Railway repair works in the town in the early 1900s. As well as swimming and Turkish baths, the building housed numerous medical services including a dispensary. Sally Hawson from Save Swindon's Heritage, the group that set up the petition, said: "I am delighted that this building has finally got the listing it deserves and the recognition that it is a unique gem that merits protection for future generations. "I am hopeful that this new grading will now provide the protection this building has been crying out for and that greater respect for the building will now be enforced by the council." In June, Historic England and the local authority announced they would jointly invest £7.6m in regeneration works which included improving the condition of the hydro. Dale Heenan, from Swindon Borough Council, said: "The council welcomes the outside recognition that this building is now of national importance and is of special interest, warranting every effort to preserve it." In 2016, Greenwich Leisure Limited announced it would conduct a feasibility study into "the best way to secure the future of the Hydro for generations to come". At the time it said the building "has numerous long-term maintenance issues, resulting in the hydro currently operating at a loss". The company has been approached by the BBC for comment.
instances.output: Swindon's Health Hydro gets new Grade II* listed status

instances.input: Raphaël André, 51, was pronounced dead early on Sunday after being discovered in a portable toilet next to a centre he had stayed at the day before. The Open Door shelter, normally open 24 hours, had been forced to close by Montreal health officials. Advocates for the homeless say Mr André may have frozen to death. The cause of death has not been confirmed by officials. A friend discovered his body at around 03:00 EST (08:00 GMT) on Sunday. Police have determined Mr André's death was not criminal and have turned the investigation over to the coroner. Mr André was a member of the Innu nation, initially from the Matimekush-Lac John community in northern Quebec, according to CBC News. He was a regular at the Open Door shelter, a drop-in centre in central Montreal that provides services to unhoused people, staff told Canadian media. He had stopped by for a meal and a shower on Saturday night and had been seen that day building snowmen nearby. Montreal temperatures dropped below freezing on Saturday evening into Sunday morning. Open Door executive director Mélodie Racine told CBC that the province's health agency had forced the shelter to close its doors at 21:30 local time on Sunday following a Covid-19 outbreak. "He didn't die for one reason," Ms Racine said. "There are a lot of factors in place. But what I know is that if he was not asked to leave, he would have probably stayed inside." Montreal public health officials said in a statement it recommended Open Door be reopened once the outbreak was contained, and Mayor Valérie Plante said the city would work with public agencies to keep the shelter open through the night. The shelter did not immediately respond to a request for comment from the BBC.
instances.output: Homeless Montreal man dies in cold just yards from shelter

            pronoun_index = i
            break

    # If all checks pass, return True

instances.input: CONTEXT: I don't think you can any better than light brown soft sugar!It has a similar taste, similar moisture content (though palm sugar varies hugely in moisture content from a thick honey texture to a solid block), similar color and caramelizes in a similar way.An ever closer option is jaggery, which is almost identical to palm sugar, just made from sugar cane rather than palms. It is probably more difficult to get hold of though, and the difference is rarely noticeable in a finished dish, so I would say light brown sugar is your best option  <sep>  Q: What can I substitute for palm sugar? A: I don't think you can any better than light brown soft sugar  Q: Does honey also make a good substitute? A: An ever closer option is jaggery, which is almost identical to palm sugar, just made from sugar cane rather than palms  Q: Is jaggery like granulated sugar? A: almost identical to palm sugar, just made from sugar cane rather than palms  <sep> FOLLOWUP_Q: What are some other good substitutes?
instances.inspect_output: A: Other good substitutes for palm sugar include coconut sugar, which has a similar flavor profile, and brown sugar, which can also work well in recipes. Maple syrup can be used in some cases, but it will add a different flavor. If you're looking for a more natural option, you might consider using date sugar, which is made from dried dates and has a rich, caramel-like taste.
instances.mapper_output: What can I substitute for palm sugar?
instances.control_output:
Some other good substitutes for palm sugar include:

1. **Coconut sugar** - Similar in flavor and texture to palm sugar, it is made from the sap of coconut palms.
2. **Brown sugar** - Light or dark brown sugar can work well due to its molasses content, which gives it a similar taste.
3. **Maple syrup** - While it has a different flavor profile, it can provide sweetness and moisture in recipes.
4. **Agave nectar** - A liquid sweetener that can be used in place of palm sugar, though it has a different taste.
5. **Date sugar** - Made from ground dried dates, it has a rich flavor and can be a good alternative.

These substitutes may vary in sweetness and moisture content, so adjustments in quantity may be necessary.

instances.input: CONTEXT: Refrigerating cooked potatoes is perfectly safe. The myriad of frozen and refrigerated potato products on the market demonstrate this, as do the experiences of millions of home cooks.In fact, it is raw potatoes that should not be refrigerated, not because of safety concerns, but because they will convert starches to sugars and taste oddly sweet. The ideal storage temperature is slightly higher, 45-50 F.  <sep>  Q: Is it bad to refrigerate leftover potatoes? A: Refrigerating cooked potatoes is perfectly safe  <sep> FOLLOWUP_Q: What is the best way to reheat them?
instances.inspect_output: A: The best way to reheat leftover cooked potatoes is to use an oven or a skillet. If using an oven, preheat it to 350°F (175°C) and spread the potatoes on a baking sheet, heating them for about 15-20 minutes until warmed through. If using a skillet, add a little oil or butter and heat over medium heat, stirring occasionally until they are heated to your liking. You can also microwave them, but this may result in a softer texture.
instances.mapper_output: What is the best way to reheat them?
instances.control_output: The best way to reheat leftover cooked potatoes is to use an oven or a skillet. If using an oven, preheat it to 350°F (175°C) and spread the potatoes on a baking sheet, covering them with foil to retain moisture. Heat for about 15-20 minutes, or until warmed through. If using a skillet, add a little oil or butter over medium heat and cook the potatoes, stirring occasionally, until they are heated through and crispy on the outside. You can also reheat them in the microwave, but this may result in a softer texture.

instances.input: CONTEXT: I'd say freeze nearly all of them uncooked and bake when ready. That way they go through only one cooking and maintain the fresh lasagna taste/feel.The sauce and and the cheese will freeze ok. Mozzarella is a pretty sensitive cheese and once it's been baked, it's not going to hold as well when thawed and re-warmed. In my experience it gets gummy and the fat runs off. You have much better chance of enjoying a good meal with the cheese frozen raw.Unfortunately, your noodles will likely take the hardest hit, but will likely fare better than going through two heat cycles.The ground beef and sausage will also fare better that way.The reason I suggested freezing MOST and not all, is that there are times when you're behind the 8-ball, hungry, and don't have the time to bake the sucker, and even ponder settling for dog-food. At that point having a silver bullet in the freezer is a bonus ;) and you'll be ok with inferior lasagna.  <sep>  Q: Should homemade lasagna be frozen cooked or uncooked?
instances.inspect_output: Homemade lasagna should be frozen uncooked. This way, it goes through only one cooking cycle, which helps maintain the fresh taste and texture. The sauce and cheese freeze better when raw, and the noodles, while they may not hold up perfectly, will fare better than if they were cooked first.
instances.mapper_output: Should homemade lasagna be frozen cooked or uncooked?
instances.control_output: Homemade lasagna should be frozen uncooked. This way, it goes through only one cooking cycle, which helps maintain the fresh taste and texture. The sauce and cheese freeze well, but cooked mozzarella can become gummy and lose quality when thawed and reheated. Freezing uncooked lasagna also helps the noodles and meat fare better. However, it's also good to have some cooked lasagna on hand for convenience when you're short on time.

instances.inspect_output: A: Making the biscuits fairly thick helps prevent crumbling, but it should not significantly affect the taste of the shortbread. The texture may be slightly different, but the flavor should remain intact as long as they are stored properly.

instances.input: CONTEXT: First off, the ISO standard is not intended to produce a good cup of tea. It is designed to produce a consistent one for taste testing, so that no tea manufacturer can claim that his tea wasn't made "properly".It's title is "Tea -- Preparation of liquor for use in sensory tests"As for the actual tea making, yes, leaving the bag in longer will make a stronger cup of tea. The concentration of caffeine (along with flavour molecules and everything else) will slowly trend towards an equal concentration in the leaf and in the water. The longer you leave the tea bag/leaves in the water, the closer to equilibrium you will get.There are other factors that affect this, such as the temperature of the water, cut of the leaves, bag versus loose leaf and so on, but the trend is always towards equilibrium as time progresses.I'm not sure where the upper limit of this lies, but I think that once the cup is cold there's no point in it anyway. Thus, leaving the bag in for an hour is a bit much. I usually steep my tea for 3-6 minutes, depending on how strong I want it to be.  <sep>  Q: Does brewing tea for longer result in higher caffeine content? A: leaving the bag in longer will make a stronger cup of tea  Q: How much? A: I usually steep my tea for 3-6 minutes, depending on how strong I want it to be  <sep> FOLLOWUP_Q: What about 1 hour or 1 day
instances.soft_output: No {Negotiator
instances.inspect_output: Brewing tea for 1 hour or 1 day will likely result in a very strong cup of tea, but it may also lead to over-extraction, which can make the tea taste bitter and unpleasant. The optimal steeping time is generally around 3-6 minutes, as this allows for a good balance of flavor and caffeine without the negative effects of over-extraction. After a certain point, especially if the tea cools down, the benefits of steeping for an extended period diminish.
instances.control_output: Brewing tea for 1 hour or even 1 day will likely result in a very strong cup of tea, but it may also lead to undesirable flavors and bitterness. The extraction of caffeine and other compounds continues as long as the tea is in the water, but after a certain point, especially beyond 6 minutes, the tea can become overly astringent and unpleasant. Additionally, once the tea cools down, the enjoyment of the flavor diminishes, making such long steeping times less practical. It's generally recommended to stick to the 3-6 minute range for optimal flavor and caffeine extraction.

instances.input: CONTEXT: Chocolate is an odd substance when it comes to melting and hardening. When chocolate hardens, its melting point will end up being just a few degrees higher than the hardening temperature.When chocolate crystallizes at high temperatures, it forms a strong, dense crystalline structure that, texture-wise, is quite brittle. Most bought chocolate (baker's chocolate and chips) is already tempered, but when you melt it, you break down that crystalline structure, and if you harden it at room temperature then it ends up forming very weak crystals that will melt in your hand.If you use a special coating chocolate (couverture or the lower-quality compound chocolate) then you don't need to worry so much about tempering (although it's still a good idea), but if you use ordinary chocolate and don't temper it then your coating will end up being messy and wet and rub off on your hands while you eat it. It might even melt while stored.Tempering is the key to making a room-temperature-stable chocolate coating. If you don't do this, you'll need to keep your pastries refrigerated until they're ready to consume.  <sep>  Q: What is the purpose of tempering chocolate? A: Tempering is the key to making a room-temperature-stable chocolate coating  <sep> FOLLOWUP_Q: Can I just melt chocolate rather than tempering?
instances.inspect_output: A: If you just melt chocolate without tempering, the resulting coating will be messy and wet, and it may rub off on your hands while you eat it. Additionally, it might melt while stored, so tempering is important for achieving a stable chocolate coating at room temperature.
instances.mapper_output: Can I just melt chocolate rather than tempering?
instances.control_output: A: While you can melt chocolate without tempering, it will not create a stable coating. The melted chocolate may form weak crystals when it hardens, leading to a messy and wet texture that can melt easily in your hands. Tempering is necessary to achieve a stable chocolate coating that can be stored at room temperature without issues.

instances.input: CONTEXT: Caffeine is bitter, tea isn't too bitter because as you mentioned it's only got 40mg of caffeine. If you then triple the amount in there it's going to taste that way. Try adding 3 tea bags to your cup and I'm certain it'll taste just as bitter.Energy drinks have copious amounts of sweeteners added hence why they don't taste like caffeine. If you are hell bent on more caffeine without the associated bitterness but not adding sugar maybe a sweetener like stevia would be the way forward...  <sep>  Q: Why does caffeine taste bitter when added to tea? A: Caffeine is bitter  Q: Why doesn't caffeine that is already in the tea or in an energy drink taste bitter? A: Energy drinks have copious amounts of sweeteners added hence why they don't taste like caffeine  Q: How should caffeine be added to drinkable fluids so it has no taste? A: Try adding 3 tea bags to your cup and I'm certain it'll taste just as bitter  Q: What can be done to make it taste better without adding lots of sugar? A: If you are hell bent on more caffeine without the associated bitterness but not adding sugar maybe a sweetener like stevia would be the way forward  <sep> FOLLOWUP_Q: How much caffeine is in different kinds of tea naturally?
instances.inspect_output: A: The caffeine content in different types of tea can vary. Generally, black tea contains about 40-70 mg of caffeine per 8 oz cup, green tea has about 20-45 mg, oolong tea has around 30-50 mg, and white tea typically has about 15-30 mg. Herbal teas usually contain little to no caffeine.
instances.mapper_output: How much caffeine is in different kinds of tea naturally?
instances.control_output:
The amount of caffeine in different kinds of tea can vary significantly. Here are some general estimates for the caffeine content in an 8-ounce (240 ml) serving of various types of tea:

1. **Black Tea**: Approximately 40-70 mg of caffeine.
2. **Green Tea**: Approximately 20-45 mg of caffeine.
3. **Oolong Tea**: Approximately 30-50 mg of caffeine.
4. **White Tea**: Approximately 15-30 mg of caffeine.
5. **Herbal Tea**: Generally caffeine-free, but some blends may contain small amounts.

These values can vary based on factors such as the type of tea leaves used, the brewing time, and the temperature of the water.

instances.input: CONTEXT: Generally speaking, maple syrup will work fine in any recipe that calls for honey. They may have slightly different viscosities and water content, but that could be true between two honeys as well, so I wouldn't worry about it a lot. When substituting with maple syrup, it's important to consider whether the strong flavor of the maple syrup will complement the other ingredients in the recipe.

instances.inspect_output:
The premise states that the sun is the ultimate source of energy in most ecosystems and for almost all living things. The hypothesis claims that most of the energy used by living things comes either directly or indirectly from the sun. 

The hypothesis logically follows from the premise, as it summarizes the idea that the sun provides energy that supports life, either directly (through processes like photosynthesis) or indirectly (through food chains). 

Therefore, the relationship between the premise and the hypothesis is that the hypothesis is supported by the premise. 

Final answer: The hypothesis is supported by the premise.
instances.control_output: The hypothesis is supported by the premise.

Premise: Gut flora produce compounds such as indole, skatole, and thiols (sulfur-containing compounds), as well as the inorganic gas hydrogen sulfide. 
 Hypothesis: Hydrogen sulfide is a noxious and toxic gas produced from decaying organic matter that contains sulfur.
instances.soft_output:
entails entails
Explanation: The statement that hydrogen sulfide is a noxious and toxic gas is supported by the information in the premise. The premise lists hydrogen sulfide as one of the compounds produced by gut flora, which suggests that it is indeed a product of organic matter decomposition. Therefore, the hypothesis is entailed by the premise. 

This question requires inductive reasoning as it involves making an inference about the nature of hydrogen sulfide based on the information provided. Premise: Like fish and amphibians, reptiles are ectothermic animals, which is to say that they produce very little metabolic heat and their body temperature depends essentially on that of the environment. 
 Hypothesis: Reptiles are described by the term ectothermic, which means their internal temperature depends on the temperature of their environment.
instances.soft_output:
entails entails
Explanation: This question requires inductive reasoning, as it asks the reader to make an inference based on the information provided. The premise states that reptiles, like fish and amphibians, are ectothermic animals, which means their body temperature is dependent on their environment. The hypothesis states that reptiles are described by the term ectothermic, which also means their internal temperature depends on the temperature of their environment. mapper_verbalization:
Classify each given tweet as "entailment", "neutral", or "contradiction" based on its relation to the target tweet. Use the following labels:
- "entailment" if the tweet is a paraphrase or a slight variation of the target.
- "neutral" if the tweet has no relation to the target or expresses neither an entailing nor a contradictory view.
- "contradiction" if the tweet expresses an idea that is against the target sentence.

 Person2:  It leaves at 8: 30 .  
 Person1:      I see . Are there any seats available ?  
 Person2:  Just a moment , please . Yes , you can have a seat .  
 Person1:      Good . How much is a one-way ticket ?  
 Person2:  It's 38 dollars .  
 Person1:      All right . Here's 40 dollars .  
 Person2:  Here's your ticket and change .  
 Person1:      Thank you . Which gate should I go to for the bus ?  
 Person2:  Go to gate number 2 , please .  
 Person1:      Thank you very much .  
 Person2:  Don't mention it .
instances.inspect_output:
**Conversation Summary:**

- **Person1** inquires about the bus schedule to Boston.
- **Person2** informs them that the next bus leaves at 8:30.
- **Person1** asks about seat availability, and **Person2** confirms there are seats available.
- **Person1** requests the price for a one-way ticket, which is $38.
- **Person1** pays with $40 and receives their ticket and change.
- **Person1** asks for the gate number for the bus, and **Person2** directs them to gate number 2.
- **Person1** expresses gratitude, and **Person2** responds politely.

hard_prompt: In this task, you are given a part of an article. Your task is to generate headline (title) for this text. Preferred headlines are under fifteen words.

instances.input: We adress the problem of dueling bandits defined on partially ordered sets, or posets. In this setting, arms may not be comparable, and there may be several (incomparable) optimal arms. We propose an algorithm, UnchainedBandits, that efficiently finds the set of optimal arms of any poset even when pairs of comparable arms cannot be distinguished from pairs of incomparable arms, with a set of minimal assumptions. This algorithm relies on the concept of decoys, which stems from social psychology. For the easier case where the incomparability information may be accessible, we propose a second algorithm, SlicingBandits, which takes advantage of this information and achieves a very significant gain of performance compared to UnchainedBandits. We provide theoretical guarantees and experimental evaluation for both algorithms.
instances.output: Decoy Bandits Dueling on a Poset
instances.soft_output: assistantassistant

The authors introduce an algorithm named UnchainedBandits, designed to effectively identify the optimal arms within any poset, even when distinguishing between comparable and incomparable arms is not possible, under minimal assumptions. Additionally, they present a second algorithm, SlicingBandits, which is applicable when incomparability information is available, allowing it to significantly outperform UnchainedBandits. The authors support their claims with theoretical guarantees and experimental results for both algorithms.
instances.mapper_output: The paper presents two algorithms, UnchainedBandits and SlicingBandits, for solving dueling bandits on partially ordered sets, efficiently identifying optimal arms with minimal assumptions and providing theoretical guarantees and experimental evaluations.

instances.input: We present a new model for prediction markets, in which we use risk measures to model agents and introduce a market maker to describe the trading process. This specific choice on modelling tools brings us mathematical convenience. The analysis shows that the whole market effectively approaches a global objective, despite that the market is designed such that each agent only cares about its own goal. Additionally, the market dynamics provides a sensible algorithm for optimising the global objective. An intimate connection between machine learning and our markets is thus established, such that we could 1) analyse a market by applying machine learning methods to the global objective, and 2) solve machine learning problems by setting up and running certain markets.
instances.output: Multi-period Trading Prediction Markets with Connections to Machine Learning
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output: The new model for prediction markets incorporates risk measures to represent agents and introduces a market maker to facilitate trading. This approach offers mathematical convenience and reveals that the market can effectively converge towards a global objective, even though each agent is primarily focused on its individual goals. The dynamics of the market also yield a practical algorithm for optimizing the global objective. Furthermore, a close relationship between machine learning and these markets is established, allowing for two key applications: analyzing a market through machine learning techniques applied to the global objective, and addressing machine learning challenges by creating and operating specific markets.
instances.mapper_output: This paper introduces a prediction market model using risk measures and a market maker, demonstrating how individual agent goals align with a global objective, linking machine learning and market analysis.
instances.fs_output: Multi-period Trading Prediction Markets with Connections to Machine Learning
instances.control_output: We introduce a novel model for prediction markets that incorporates risk measures to represent agents and features a market maker to facilitate the trading process. Furthermore, the dynamics of the market yield a coherent algorithm for optimizing the global objective. instances.soft2_output: A Risk-Based Model for Prediction Markets

instances.input: Recently deep neural networks (DNNs) have been used to learn speaker features. However, the quality of the learned features is not sufficiently good, so a complex back-end model, either neural or probabilistic, has to be used to address the residual uncertainty when applied to speaker verification, just as with raw features. This paper presents a convolutional timedelay deep neural network structure (CT-DNN) for speaker feature learning. Our experimental results on the Fisher database demonstrated that this CT-DNN can produce highquality speaker features: even with a single feature (0.3 seconds including the context), the EER can be as low as 7.68%. This effectively confirmed that the speaker trait is largely a deterministic short-time property rather than a long-time distributional pattern, and therefore can be extracted from just dozens of frames.
instances.output: Deep Speaker Feature Learning for Text-independent Speaker Verification
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output: The paper discusses the use of convolutional time-delay deep neural networks (CT-DNNs) for learning speaker features, addressing the limitations of traditional deep neural networks (DNNs) in speaker verification tasks. Despite the advancements in DNNs, the features they learn often require complex back-end models to manage residual uncertainties. The CT-DNN structure proposed in this study shows promising results, achieving a low equal error rate (EER) of 7.68% on the Fisher database with just a single feature that includes a short context of 0.3 seconds. This finding suggests that speaker characteristics are primarily determined by short-time properties rather than long-term patterns, allowing for effective feature extraction from only a few frames.
instances.mapper_output: This paper introduces a convolutional time-delay deep neural network (CT-DNN) for speaker feature learning, achieving high-quality features with low error rates, confirming speaker traits as short-time deterministic properties.
instances.fs_output: Deep Speaker Feature Learning for Text-independent Speaker Verification
instances.control_output: The paper introduces a convolutional time-delay deep neural network (CT-DNN) designed for learning speaker features, addressing the limitations of traditional deep neural networks (DNNs) in speaker verification tasks. Despite the advancements in DNNs, the quality of the features they produce often necessitates the use of complex back-end models to manage residual uncertainties. The CT-DNN structure demonstrated significant improvements in feature quality, as evidenced by experimental results on the Fisher database, where a single feature (spanning 0.3 seconds of context) achieved an equal error rate (EER) as low as 7.68%. This finding supports the notion that speaker characteristics are primarily deterministic short-time properties, which can be effectively captured using only a limited number of frames.
instances.soft2_output: 0.3 Seconds is Enough for Speaker Verification

instances.input: We present a deep hierarchical recurrent neural network for sequence tagging. Given a sequence of words, our model employs deep gated recurrent units on both character and word levels to encode morphology and context information, and applies a conditional random field layer to predict the tags. Our model is task independent, language independent, and feature engineering free. We further extend our model to multi-task and crosslingual joint training by sharing the architecture and parameters. Our model achieves state-of-the-art results in multiple languages on several benchmark tasks including POS tagging, chunking, and NER. We also demonstrate that multi-task and cross-lingual joint training can improve the performance in various cases.
instances.output: Multi-Task Cross-Lingual Sequence Tagging from Scratch
instances.soft_output: assistant
instances.inspect_output:
The presented model is a deep hierarchical recurrent neural network designed for sequence tagging tasks. It utilizes deep gated recurrent units at both the character and word levels to effectively capture morphological and contextual information from sequences of words. To make predictions about tags, the model incorporates a conditional random field layer. Notably, this model is versatile, being task-independent, language-independent, and requiring no feature engineering.

Additionally, the model can be extended to support multi-task and cross-lingual joint training by sharing its architecture and parameters across different tasks and languages. This approach has led to state-of-the-art performance across multiple languages on various benchmark tasks, including part-of-speech (POS) tagging, chunking, and named entity recognition (NER). The results also indicate that employing multi-task and cross-lingual joint training can enhance performance in numerous scenarios.
instances.mapper_output: We propose a deep hierarchical recurrent neural network for sequence tagging, utilizing gated recurrent units and conditional random fields, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple languages and tasks, including POS tagging and NER.
instances.fs_output: Deep Hierarchical Recurrent Neural Network for Sequence Tagging
instances.control_output: We introduce a deep hierarchical recurrent neural network designed for sequence tagging tasks. Our model utilizes deep gated recurrent units at both the character and word levels to effectively capture morphological and contextual information. To predict tags, we incorporate a conditional random field layer. Notably, our model is independent of specific tasks, languages, and does not require feature engineering. Additionally, we enhance our model's capabilities through multi-task and cross-lingual joint training by sharing both architecture and parameters. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple languages on various benchmark tasks, including part-of-speech tagging, chunking, and named entity recognition (NER). Furthermore, we provide evidence that multi-task and cross-lingual joint training can lead to performance improvements in numerous scenarios.
instances.soft2_output: Deep Hierarchical Recurrent Neural Networks for Sequence Tagging

instances.input: This manuscript presents a trust computation for international relations and its calculus, which related to Bayesian inference, Dempster-Shafer theory and subjective logic. We proposed a method that allows a trust computation which is previously subjective and incomputable. An example of case study for the trust computation is the United States of America–Great Britain relations. The method supports decision makers in a government such as foreign ministry, defense ministry, presidential or prime minister office. The Department of Defense (DoD) may use our method to determine a nation that can be known as a friendly, neutral or hostile nation.
instances.output: A Mathematical Trust Algebra for International Nation Relations Computation and Evaluation
instances.soft_output: assistantassistant
instances.inspect_output: This manuscript introduces a novel approach to trust computation in international relations, integrating concepts from Bayesian inference, Dempster-Shafer theory, and subjective logic. We propose a method that transforms previously subjective and incomputable trust assessments into quantifiable metrics. A case study exemplifying this trust computation is the relationship between the United States and Great Britain. Our method is designed to assist decision-makers in government sectors, including foreign affairs, defense, and executive offices. The Department of Defense (DoD) can utilize our approach to classify nations as friendly, neutral, or hostile, thereby enhancing strategic decision-making.
instances.mapper_output: This paper introduces a trust computation method for international relations, utilizing Bayesian inference and Dempster-Shafer theory, aiding decision-makers in assessing nations' trustworthiness, exemplified by US-Great Britain relations.
instances.fs_output: Trust Computation in International Relations Using Bayesian Inference and Dempster-Shafer Theory
instances.control_output: This manuscript introduces a novel approach to trust computation in international relations, integrating concepts from Bayesian inference, Dempster-Shafer theory, and subjective logic. We propose a method that transforms previously subjective and incomputable trust assessments into a structured computation. A case study focusing on the relations between the United States and Great Britain illustrates the application of our method. This approach is designed to assist decision-makers in government sectors, including foreign affairs, defense, and executive offices. The Department of Defense (DoD) can utilize our method to classify nations as friendly, neutral, or hostile, thereby enhancing strategic decision-making.

instances.input: Syllogism is a type of deductive reasoning involving quantified statements. The syllogistic reasoning scheme in the classical Aristotelian framework involves three crisp term sets and four linguistic quantifiers, for which the main support is the linguistic properties of the quantifiers. A number of fuzzy approaches for defining an approximate syllogism have been proposed for which the main support is cardinality calculus. In this paper we analyze fuzzy syllogistic models previously described by Zadeh and Dubois et al. and compare their behavior with that of the classical Aristotelian framework to check which of the 24 classical valid syllogistic reasoning patterns (called moods) are particular crisp cases of these fuzzy approaches. This allows us to assess to what extent these approaches can be considered as either plausible extensions of the classical crisp syllogism or a basis for a general approach to the problem of approximate syllogism.
instances.output: On the analysis of set-based fuzzy quantified reasoning using classical syllogistics
instances.soft_output: A Comparative Study of Classical and Fuzzy Syllogisms: Which Fuzzy Syllogisms Are Particular Cases of the Classical Aristotelian Framework?
instances.inspect_output: The paper discusses the concept of syllogism, a form of deductive reasoning that utilizes quantified statements. It contrasts classical Aristotelian syllogistic reasoning, which is based on three clear term sets and four linguistic quantifiers, with fuzzy approaches that have been developed to create an approximate version of syllogism. The fuzzy models, as proposed by researchers like Zadeh and Dubois et al., rely on cardinality calculus rather than strict linguistic properties. The authors analyze these fuzzy syllogistic models and compare them to the classical framework to determine which of the 24 valid syllogistic patterns (or moods) from Aristotle can be seen as specific instances of these fuzzy approaches. This comparison aims to evaluate whether the fuzzy models can be viewed as reasonable extensions of traditional syllogism or as foundational methods for addressing the concept of approximate syllogism.
instances.mapper_output: This paper analyzes fuzzy syllogistic models by Zadeh and Dubois, comparing them to classical Aristotelian syllogism to evaluate their validity and potential as extensions of traditional reasoning patterns.
instances.fs_output: Fuzzy Syllogistic Reasoning: Analyzing Extensions of Classical Deductive Patterns
instances.control_output: This paper examines fuzzy syllogistic models proposed by Zadeh and Dubois et al., comparing their behavior to classical Aristotelian syllogism. It focuses on the relationship between fuzzy approaches and the 24 valid syllogistic patterns (moods) in classical logic, assessing whether these fuzzy models serve as plausible extensions of traditional syllogism or provide a foundation for understanding approximate syllogism. The analysis highlights the differences in reasoning between crisp and fuzzy frameworks, emphasizing the role of linguistic quantifiers and cardinality calculus in fuzzy logic.
instances.soft2_output: 24 Classical Syllogistic Moods as Particular Cases of Fuzzy Syllogisms

instances.input: The family of temporal difference (TD) methods span a spectrum from computationally frugal linear methods like TD(λ) to data efficient least squares methods. Least square methods make the best use of available data directly computing the TD solution and thus do not require tuning a typically highly sensitive learning rate parameter, but require quadratic computation and storage. Recent algorithmic developments have yielded several sub-quadratic methods that use an approximation to the least squares TD solution, but incur bias. In this paper, we propose a new family of accelerated gradient TD (ATD) methods that (1) provide similar data efficiency benefits to least-squares methods, at a fraction of the computation and storage (2) significantly reduce parameter sensitivity compared to linear TD methods, and (3) are asymptotically unbiased. We illustrate these claims with a proof of convergence in expectation and experiments on several benchmark domains and a large-scale industrial energy allocation domain.
instances.soft_output:
assistant

http://www.jmlr.org/papers/volume12/maei11a/maei11a.pdf Accelerated Gradient Temporal Difference Learning for Policy Evaluation and Selection in Complex Domains
instances.inspect_output:
The text discusses a new family of accelerated gradient temporal difference (ATD) methods in the context of reinforcement learning. It highlights the advantages of these methods over traditional temporal difference (TD) methods and least squares methods. Here’s a summary of the key points:

1. **TD Methods Overview**: TD methods range from simple, computationally efficient methods like TD(λ) to more data-efficient least squares methods. The latter directly compute the TD solution but require significant computational resources and careful tuning of the learning rate.

2. **Challenges with Least Squares**: While least squares methods are efficient in utilizing data, they demand quadratic computation and storage, which can be a limitation.

3. **Recent Developments**: There have been advancements in sub-quadratic methods that approximate the least squares TD solution but introduce bias.

4. **Proposed ATD Methods**: The paper introduces ATD methods that:
   - Offer data efficiency similar to least squares methods but with reduced computational and storage requirements.
   - Exhibit lower sensitivity to parameter tuning compared to linear TD methods.
   - Maintain asymptotic unbiasedness.

5. **Validation**: The authors provide a proof of convergence in expectation and present experimental results across various benchmark domains, including a large-scale industrial energy allocation scenario, to support their claims.

This summary encapsulates the essence of the proposed methods and their significance in the field of reinforcement learning.
instances.mapper_output: This paper introduces accelerated gradient TD (ATD) methods, offering data efficiency and reduced parameter sensitivity compared to traditional methods, while maintaining asymptotic unbiasedness and demonstrating effectiveness through theoretical proofs and experiments.
instances.fs_output: Accelerated Gradient Methods for Temporal Difference Learning
instances.control_output: In this paper, we introduce a new family of accelerated gradient temporal difference (ATD) methods that aim to combine the advantages of least squares methods with improved computational efficiency and reduced parameter sensitivity. Our ATD methods achieve similar data efficiency to least squares methods while significantly lowering the computational and storage requirements. Additionally, they exhibit reduced sensitivity to parameter tuning compared to traditional linear TD methods and maintain asymptotic unbiasedness. We support our claims with a proof of convergence in expectation and conduct experiments across various benchmark domains, including a large-scale industrial energy allocation scenario.

instances.input: Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) on Electronic Health Records (EHR) data is widely-used for early detection of diseases. Classical LDA for EHR data classification, however, suffers from two handicaps: the ill-posed estimation of LDA parameters (e.g., covariance matrix), and the “linear inseparability” of EHR data. To handle these two issues, in this paper, we propose a novel classifier FWDA — Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis, that makes predictions in an ensemble way. Specifically, FWDA first surrogates the distribution of inverse covariance matrices using a Wishart distribution estimated from the training data, then “weightedaverages” the classification results of multiple LDA classifiers parameterized by the sampled inverse covariance matrices via a Bayesian Voting scheme. The weights for voting are optimally updated to adapt each new input data, so as to enable the nonlinear classification. Theoretical analysis indicates that FWDA possesses a fast convergence rate and a robust performance on high dimensional data. Extensive experiments on large-scale EHR dataset show that our approach outperforms stateof-the-art algorithms by a large margin.
instances.output: FWDA: a Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis with its Application to Electronic Health Records Data Classification
instances.inspect_output: The paper introduces a new classifier called Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis (FWDA) designed to improve the classification of Electronic Health Records (EHR) data, addressing two main challenges: the poor estimation of LDA parameters and the linear inseparability of EHR data. FWDA utilizes a Wishart distribution to estimate the inverse covariance matrices from training data, allowing it to create multiple LDA classifiers. These classifiers then make predictions that are combined through a Bayesian Voting scheme, where the weights for each classifier are dynamically updated based on new input data, facilitating nonlinear classification. Theoretical analysis suggests that FWDA converges quickly and performs robustly with high-dimensional data. Experimental results demonstrate that FWDA significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on large-scale EHR datasets.
instances.mapper_output: The paper introduces Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis (FWDA), a novel classifier for Electronic Health Records that addresses LDA's limitations, enhancing disease detection through ensemble predictions and improved parameter estimation.
instances.fs_output: Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis for Electronic Health Records Classification
instances.control_output:
In this paper, we introduce a novel classifier called Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis (FWDA) to address the challenges faced by classical Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) when applied to Electronic Health Records (EHR) data. Traditional LDA struggles with the ill-posed estimation of parameters, such as the covariance matrix, and the linear inseparability of EHR data. 

FWDA tackles these issues by employing an ensemble approach. It begins by modeling the distribution of inverse covariance matrices using a Wishart distribution derived from the training data. Subsequently, FWDA aggregates the classification results from multiple LDA classifiers, each parameterized by different sampled inverse covariance matrices, through a Bayesian Voting scheme. The voting weights are dynamically updated to adapt to new input data, facilitating nonlinear classification.

Theoretical analysis demonstrates that FWDA achieves a rapid convergence rate and maintains robust performance, particularly in high-dimensional settings. Comprehensive experiments conducted on large-scale EHR datasets reveal that FWDA significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art algorithms, showcasing its effectiveness for early disease detection.
instances.soft2_output: Fast Wishart Discriminant Analysis for Early Detection of Diseases from Electronic Health Records

instances.input: We propose a method for learning expressive energy-based policies for continuous states and actions, which has been feasible only in tabular domains before. We apply our method to learning maximum entropy policies, resulting into a new algorithm, called soft Q-learning, that expresses the optimal policy via a Boltzmann distribution. We use the recently proposed amortized Stein variational gradient descent to learn a stochastic sampling network that approximates samples from this distribution. The benefits of the proposed algorithm include improved exploration and compositionality that allows transferring skills between tasks, which we confirm in simulated experiments with swimming and walking robots. We also draw a connection to actorcritic methods, which can be viewed performing approximate inference on the corresponding energy-based model.
instances.output: Reinforcement Learning with Deep Energy-Based Policies
instances.soft_output: Learning to Search in Continuous Spaces with Amortized Stein Variational Gradient Descent∗}
instances.inspect_output: The proposed method introduces a novel approach for learning expressive energy-based policies in continuous state and action spaces, a challenge previously limited to tabular domains. This method leads to the development of a new algorithm named soft Q-learning, which formulates the optimal policy using a Boltzmann distribution. To approximate samples from this distribution, we utilize the recently introduced amortized Stein variational gradient descent to create a stochastic sampling network. The advantages of this algorithm include enhanced exploration capabilities and compositionality, facilitating skill transfer across different tasks. These benefits are validated through simulated experiments involving swimming and walking robots. Additionally, we establish a link to actor-critic methods, interpreting them as performing approximate inference on the associated energy-based model.
instances.mapper_output: We introduce soft Q-learning, a method for learning energy-based policies in continuous domains, enhancing exploration and skill transfer in robotic tasks, and connecting to actor-critic methods for improved performance.
instances.fs_output: Soft Q-Learning: Energy-Based Policies for Continuous Control Tasks
instances.control_output: We introduce a novel approach for developing expressive energy-based policies applicable to continuous states and actions, a capability previously limited to tabular domains. Our method leads to the creation of a new algorithm, termed soft Q-learning, which formulates the optimal policy using a Boltzmann distribution. To achieve this, we leverage the recently introduced amortized Stein variational gradient descent to construct a stochastic sampling network that effectively approximates samples from the Boltzmann distribution. The advantages of our proposed algorithm include enhanced exploration and compositionality, facilitating the transfer of skills across different tasks. We validate these benefits through simulated experiments involving swimming and walking robots. Additionally, we establish a link to actor-critic methods, interpreting them as performing approximate inference on the associated energy-based model.
instances.soft2_output: Learning Energy-Based Policies via Amortized Stein Variational Gradient Descent

instances.input: Colorization of grayscale images has been a hot topic in computer vision. Previous research mainly focuses on producing a colored image to match the original one. However, since many colors share the same gray value, an input grayscale image could be diversely colored while maintaining its reality. In this paper, we design a novel solution for unsupervised diverse colorization. Specifically, we leverage conditional generative adversarial networks to model the distribution of real-world item colors, in which we develop a fully convolutional generator with multi-layer noise to enhance diversity, with multi-layer condition concatenation to maintain reality, and with stride 1 to keep spatial information. With such a novel network architecture, the model yields highly competitive performance on the open LSUN bedroom dataset. The Turing test of 80 humans further indicates our generated color schemes are highly convincible.
instances.output: Unsupervised Diverse Colorization via Generative Adversarial Networks
instances.soft_output: Fully Convolutional Generative Adversarial Networks for Diverse Colorization}
instances.inspect_output:
The paper discusses a novel approach to the colorization of grayscale images using unsupervised methods. It highlights the challenge that many colors can correspond to the same gray value, allowing for multiple valid colorizations of a single grayscale image. To address this, the authors propose a solution that utilizes conditional generative adversarial networks (GANs) to capture the distribution of colors found in real-world items.

Key features of their approach include:

1. **Fully Convolutional Generator**: The generator is designed to produce diverse color outputs while ensuring that the results remain realistic.
2. **Multi-layer Noise**: This component is introduced to enhance the diversity of the generated colorizations.
3. **Multi-layer Condition Concatenation**: This technique is used to maintain the realism of the colorized images by incorporating contextual information.
4. **Stride 1**: This design choice helps preserve spatial information in the output images.

The authors evaluate their model on the LSUN bedroom dataset, achieving competitive performance. Additionally, a Turing test involving 80 human participants suggests that the color schemes generated by their model are highly convincing, indicating the effectiveness of their approach in producing realistic and diverse colorizations.
instances.mapper_output: This paper presents an unsupervised diverse colorization method using conditional generative adversarial networks, enhancing realism and diversity in grayscale images, achieving competitive results on the LSUN bedroom dataset.
instances.fs_output: Unsupervised Diverse Colorization of Grayscale Images Using Conditional GANs
instances.control_output: In this paper, we present a novel approach to unsupervised diverse colorization of grayscale images using conditional generative adversarial networks (cGANs). Traditional methods have primarily aimed at producing a single colored image that closely matches the original, but we recognize that multiple colorizations can be valid for the same grayscale input due to the ambiguity of gray values. Our method introduces a fully convolutional generator that incorporates multi-layer noise to enhance the diversity of the generated colors. Additionally, we utilize multi-layer condition concatenation to ensure that the colorizations remain realistic and maintain spatial information through a stride of 1 in the architecture. Our approach demonstrates strong performance on the LSUN bedroom dataset, and a Turing test involving 80 human participants confirms that our generated color schemes are highly convincing.

The two sentences provide definitions related to the concept of percentage in chemistry, but they focus on slightly different aspects. 

Sentence 1 defines "percent composition" specifically in the context of the mass of each element in a compound, emphasizing the composition of a chemical substance. 

Sentence 2 refers to the term "percent or percentage" more generally, indicating it means the percentage by weight, which can apply to various contexts beyond just chemical compounds.

instances.input: Then , define your goals and work hard to achieve them .
instances.output: Then define your goals and work hard to achieve them .
instances.soft_output:
Translate
Then, define your goals and work hard to achieve them.  ###### 1. Define your goals
Set specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) goals. 
2. Break down big goals into smaller ones
Divide large goals into smaller, manageable tasks. 
3. Create an action plan
Develop a step-by-step plan to achieve your goals. 
4. Track your progress
Regularly review and adjust your progress to stay on track. 
5. Stay motivated
Celebrate your successes and learn from your failures. 
6. Seek support
Surround yourself with people who support and encourage you. 
7. Be patient and persistent
Achieving goals takes time and effort, so stay committed and focused. 
8. Review and adjust
Regularly review your goals and adjust them as needed. 
9. Stay positive and focused
Maintain a positive attitude and stay focused on your goals. 
10. Learn from failures
Use failures as opportunities to learn and grow. 
11. Stay organized
Use tools and systems to stay organized and manage your time effectively. 
12. Stay accountable
Hold yourself accountable for your actions and progress. 
13. Stay adaptable
Be willing to adjust your goals and plans as circumstances change. 
14. Stay motivated
Find ways to stay motivated and engaged throughout the process. 
15. Stay committed
Stay committed to your goals and see them through to completion. 

### Step-by-step reasoning process:
To achieve your goals, follow these steps:
1. Define your goals: Set specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) goals.
2. Break down big goals into smaller ones: Divide large goals into smaller, manageable tasks.
3. Create an action plan: Develop a step-by-step plan to achieve your goals.
4. Track your progress: Regularly review and adjust your progress to stay on track.
5. Stay motivated: Celebrate your successes and learn from your failures.
6. Seek support: Surround yourself with people who support and encourage you.
7. Be patient and persistent: Achieving goals takes time and effort, so stay committed and focused.
8. Review and adjust: Regularly review your goals and adjust them as needed.
9. Stay positive and focused: Maintain a positive attitude and stay focused on your goals.
10. Learn from failures: Use failures as opportunities to learn and grow.
11. Stay organized: Use tools and systems to stay organized and manage your time effectively.
12.

hard_prompt: In this task, you are given an abstract of article. Your task is to generate title for this article. Preferred titles are under thirty words.

instances.input: ID elements are short interspersed elements (SINEs) found in high copy number in many rodent genomes. BC1 RNA, an ID-related transcript, is derived from the single copy BC1 RNA gene. The BC1 RNA gene has been shown to be a master gene for ID element amplification in rodent genomes. ID elements are dispersed through a process termed retroposition. The retroposition process involves a number of potential regulatory steps. These regulatory steps may include transcription in the appropriate tissue, transcript stability, priming of the RNA transcript for reverse transcription and integration. This study focuses on priming of the RNA transcript for reverse transcription. BC1 RNA gene transcripts are shown to be able to prime their own reverse transcription in an efficient intramolecular and site-specific fashion. This self-priming ability is a consequence of the secondary structure of the 3'-unique region. The observation that a gene actively amplified throughout rodent evolution makes a RNA capable of efficient self-primed reverse transcription strongly suggests that self-priming is at least one feature establishing the BC1 RNA gene as a master gene for amplification of ID elements.
instances.output: BC1 RNA, the transcript from a master gene for ID element amplification, is able to prime its own reverse transcription.

instances.inspect_output:
The study discusses the role of BC1 RNA, a transcript derived from the BC1 RNA gene, in the amplification of interspersed elements (ID elements) in rodent genomes. ID elements, which are short interspersed elements (SINEs), are found in high copy numbers across many rodent species. The BC1 RNA gene is identified as a master gene that facilitates the amplification of these ID elements through a process known as retroposition.

Retroposition involves several regulatory steps, including the transcription of the gene in the appropriate tissue, the stability of the transcript, and the priming of the RNA transcript for reverse transcription and integration into the genome. This study specifically examines the priming of the RNA transcript for reverse transcription.

The findings indicate that BC1 RNA gene transcripts can efficiently self-prime their own reverse transcription in an intramolecular and site-specific manner. This self-priming capability is attributed to the secondary structure of the 3'-unique region of the RNA. The ability of BC1 RNA to self-prime suggests that this feature is significant in establishing the BC1 RNA gene as a master gene for the amplification of ID elements throughout rodent evolution.
instances.mapper_output: BC1 RNA gene transcripts are shown to be able to prime their own reverse transcription in an efficient intramolecular and site-specific fashion.
instances.fs_output: BC1 RNA gene transcripts can efficiently self-prime reverse transcription, establishing it as a master gene for ID element amplification in rodents.
instances.control_output: The BC1 RNA gene, a key player in the amplification of ID elements in rodent genomes, demonstrates a unique ability to self-prime its own reverse transcription. This self-priming capability is attributed to the secondary structure of its 3'-unique region, allowing for efficient intramolecular and site-specific reverse transcription. The study highlights the importance of this feature in the context of the gene's role as a master gene for ID element amplification, suggesting that self-priming may be a critical regulatory step in the retroposition process that contributes to the high copy number of ID elements in rodent genomes.

instances.input: DNA methylation plays an important role in biological processes in human health and disease. Recent technological advances allow unbiased whole-genome DNA methylation (methylome) analysis to be carried out on human cells. Using whole-genome bisulfite sequencing at 24.7-fold coverage (12.3-fold per strand), we report a comprehensive (92.62%) methylome and analysis of the unique sequences in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC) from the same Asian individual whose genome was deciphered in the YH project. PBMC constitute an important source for clinical blood tests world-wide. We found that 68.4% of CpG sites and <0.2% of non-CpG sites were methylated, demonstrating that non-CpG cytosine methylation is minor in human PBMC. Analysis of the PBMC methylome revealed a rich epigenomic landscape for 20 distinct genomic features, including regulatory, protein-coding, non-coding, RNA-coding, and repeat sequences. Integration of our methylome data with the YH genome sequence enabled a first comprehensive assessment of allele-specific methylation (ASM) between the two haploid methylomes of any individual and allowed the identification of 599 haploid differentially methylated regions (hDMRs) covering 287 genes. Of these, 76 genes had hDMRs within 2 kb of their transcriptional start sites of which >80% displayed allele-specific expression (ASE). These data demonstrate that ASM is a recurrent phenomenon and is highly correlated with ASE in human PBMCs. Together with recently reported similar studies, our study provides a comprehensive resource for future epigenomic research and confirms new sequencing technology as a paradigm for large-scale epigenomics studies.
instances.output: The DNA Methylome of Human Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells

instances.inspect_output:
DNA methylation is crucial for various biological processes related to human health and disease. Recent advancements in technology have enabled unbiased whole-genome DNA methylation analysis, known as methylome analysis, in human cells. In this study, we utilized whole-genome bisulfite sequencing with a coverage of 24.7-fold (12.3-fold per strand) to provide a comprehensive methylome analysis (92.62%) of unique sequences in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC) from an Asian individual whose genome was previously sequenced in the YH project. PBMCs are significant for clinical blood tests globally.

Our findings indicate that 68.4% of CpG sites and less than 0.2% of non-CpG sites were methylated, highlighting that non-CpG cytosine methylation is minimal in human PBMCs. The analysis of the PBMC methylome uncovered a diverse epigenomic landscape across 20 distinct genomic features, including regulatory elements, protein-coding genes, non-coding regions, RNA-coding sequences, and repeat sequences. By integrating our methylome data with the YH genome sequence, we conducted a comprehensive assessment of allele-specific methylation (ASM) between the two haploid methylomes of the individual, identifying 599 haploid differentially methylated regions (hDMRs) that encompass 287 genes. Notably, 76 of these genes had hDMRs located within 2 kb of their transcriptional start sites, with over 80% exhibiting allele-specific expression (ASE). 

These results indicate that ASM is a common occurrence and is closely associated with ASE in human PBMCs. Our study, alongside other recent research, offers a valuable resource for future epigenomic investigations and validates new sequencing technologies as a standard for large-scale epigenomic studies.
instances.mapper_output: DNA methylation plays an important role in biological processes in human health and disease.
instances.fs_output: The comprehensive analysis of the DNA methylome in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells reveals significant insights into allele-specific methylation and expression.

instances.input: Two human Golli (for gene expressed in the oligodendrocyte lineage)-MBP (for myelin basic protein) cDNAs have been isolated from a human oligodendroglioma cell line. Analysis of these cDNAs has enabled us to determine the entire structure of the human Golli-MBP gene. The Golli-MBP gene, which encompasses the MBP transcription unit, is approximately 179 kb in length and consists of 10 exons, seven of which constitute the MBP gene. The human Golli-MBP gene contains two transcription start sites, each of which gives rise to a family of alternatively spliced transcripts. At least two Golli-MBP transcripts, containing the first three exons of the gene and one or more MBP exons, are produced from the first transcription start site. The second family of transcripts contains only MBP exons and produces the well-known MBPs. In humans, RNA blot analysis revealed that Golli-MBP transcripts were expressed in fetal thymus, spleen, and human B-cell and macrophage cell lines, as well as in fetal spinal cord. These findings clearly link the expression of exons encoding the autoimmunogen/encephalitogen MBP in the central nervous system to cells and tissues of the immune system through normal expression of the Golli-MBP gene. They also establish that this genetic locus, which includes the MBP gene, is conserved among species, providing further evidence that the MBP transcription unit is an integral part of the Golli transcription unit and suggest that this structural arrangement is important for the genetic function and/or regulation of these genes.
instances.output: The human myelin basic protein gene is included within a 179-kilobase transcription unit: expression in the immune and central nervous systems.

instances.inspect_output:
The human Golli-MBP gene, which includes the MBP transcription unit, is approximately 179 kb long and consists of 10 exons, with seven exons dedicated to the MBP gene. It has two transcription start sites that lead to a variety of alternatively spliced transcripts. One set of transcripts includes the first three exons of the Golli-MBP gene along with one or more MBP exons, while the other set contains only MBP exons, resulting in the production of the well-known myelin basic proteins (MBPs). 

Expression analysis via RNA blotting has shown that Golli-MBP transcripts are present in several tissues, including fetal thymus, spleen, and specific human cell lines (B-cell and macrophage), as well as in fetal spinal cord. This indicates a connection between the expression of MBP exons, which are associated with autoimmune responses, and the immune system. Furthermore, the conservation of this genetic locus across species suggests that the arrangement of the Golli and MBP genes is crucial for their function and regulation.
instances.mapper_output: The Golli-MBP gene is approximately 179 kb in length and consists of 10 exons.
instances.fs_output: The human Golli-MBP gene, a 179-kilobase transcription unit, produces alternatively spliced transcripts expressed in the immune and central nervous systems.
instances.control_output: The human Golli-MBP gene, approximately 179 kb in length, consists of 10 exons, with seven exons dedicated to the MBP gene. It features two transcription start sites that generate alternatively spliced transcripts. One set of transcripts includes the first three exons of the Golli-MBP gene along with one or more MBP exons, while the other set contains only MBP exons, leading to the production of the well-known myelin basic proteins. Expression of Golli-MBP transcripts has been observed in various tissues, including fetal thymus, spleen, B-cell and macrophage cell lines, and fetal spinal cord. This expression links the MBP exons, which are associated with autoimmune responses, to the immune system and highlights the conservation of this genetic locus across species. The findings suggest that the structural arrangement of the Golli-MBP gene is crucial for its genetic function and regulation.

instances.input: Realizing the full potential of human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) and induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs) requires efficient methods for genetic modification. However, techniques to generate cell type–specific lineage reporters, as well as reliable tools to disrupt, repair or overexpress genes by gene targeting, are inefficient at best and thus are not routinely used. Here we report the highly efficient targeting of three genes in human pluripotent cells using zinc-finger nuclease (ZFN)–mediated genome editing. First, using ZFNs specific for the OCT4 (POU5F1) locus, we generated OCT4-eGFP reporter cells to monitor the pluripotent state of hESCs. Second, we inserted a transgene into the AAVS1 locus to generate a robust drug-inducible overexpression system in hESCs. Finally, we targeted the PITX3 gene, demonstrating that ZFNs can be used to generate reporter cells by targeting non-expressed genes in hESCs and hiPSCs.
instances.output: Efficient targeting of expressed and silent genes in human ESCs and iPSCs using zinc-finger nucleases

instances.inspect_output:
The efficient genetic modification of human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) and induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs) is crucial for realizing their full potential. Current methods for creating cell type-specific lineage reporters and for gene targeting—such as disrupting, repairing, or overexpressing genes—are often inefficient and not widely adopted. In this study, we demonstrate a highly effective approach using zinc-finger nuclease (ZFN)–mediated genome editing to target three specific genes in human pluripotent cells.

Firstly, we utilized ZFNs designed for the OCT4 (POU5F1) locus to create OCT4-eGFP reporter cells, which allow for the monitoring of the pluripotent state in hESCs. Secondly, we successfully inserted a transgene into the AAVS1 locus, establishing a robust drug-inducible overexpression system in hESCs. Lastly, we targeted the PITX3 gene, showcasing that ZFNs can also be employed to generate reporter cells by targeting genes that are not expressed in hESCs and hiPSCs.

instances.fs_output: Efficient gene targeting in human pluripotent stem cells using zinc-finger nucleases enables the generation of lineage reporters and overexpression systems.
instances.control_output: We present a method for efficient genetic modification of human embryonic stem cells (hESCs) and induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs) using zinc-finger nuclease (ZFN) technology. Our approach includes the successful targeting of three specific genes. Firstly, we created OCT4-eGFP reporter cells by targeting the OCT4 (POU5F1) locus, allowing for the monitoring of the pluripotent state in hESCs. Secondly, we established a robust drug-inducible overexpression system by inserting a transgene into the AAVS1 locus. Lastly, we demonstrated the versatility of ZFNs by targeting the PITX3 gene, which enabled the generation of reporter cells from non-expressed genes in both hESCs and hiPSCs. This work highlights the potential of ZFN-mediated genome editing for advancing stem cell research and applications.

instances.input: Summary. We consider the problem of comparing complex hierarchical models in which the number of parameters is not clearly defined. Using an information theoretic argument we derive a measure pD for the effective number of parameters in a model as the difference between the posterior mean of the deviance and the deviance at the posterior means of the parameters of interest. In general pD approximately corresponds to the trace of the product of Fisher's information and the posterior covariance, which in normal models is the trace of the ‘hat’ matrix projecting observations onto fitted values. Its properties in exponential families are explored. The posterior mean deviance is suggested as a Bayesian measure of fit or adequacy, and the contributions of individual observations to the fit and complexity can give rise to a diagnostic plot of deviance residuals against leverages. Adding pD to the posterior mean deviance gives a deviance information criterion for comparing models, which is related to other information criteria and has an approximate decision theoretic justification. The procedure is illustrated in some examples, and comparisons are drawn with alternative Bayesian and classical proposals. Throughout it is emphasized that the quantities required are trivial to compute in a Markov chain Monte Carlo analysis.
instances.output: Bayesian measures of model complexity and fit

The paper discusses a method for comparing complex hierarchical models where the number of parameters is not clearly defined. It introduces a measure, denoted as pD, which represents the effective number of parameters in a model. This measure is derived using an information theoretic approach, specifically as the difference between the posterior mean of the deviance and the deviance calculated at the posterior means of the parameters of interest.

In general, pD is approximately equivalent to the trace of the product of Fisher's information and the posterior covariance. In normal models, this corresponds to the trace of the 'hat' matrix that projects observations onto their fitted values. The authors explore the properties of pD within exponential families of distributions.

The posterior mean deviance is proposed as a Bayesian measure of model fit or adequacy. Additionally, the contributions of individual observations to the model's fit and complexity can be visualized through a diagnostic plot of deviance residuals against leverages.

By adding pD to the posterior mean deviance, the authors create a deviance information criterion (DIC) for model comparison. This criterion is related to other information criteria and has a decision-theoretic justification. The methodology is illustrated with examples, and comparisons are made with other Bayesian and classical approaches. The authors highlight that the necessary computations can be easily performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods.

In general, pD is approximately equivalent to the trace of the product of Fisher's information and the posterior covariance. In normal models, this corresponds to the trace of the 'hat' matrix that projects observations onto their fitted values. The authors explore the properties of pD within exponential families and propose the posterior mean deviance as a Bayesian measure of model fit or adequacy. 

Additionally, they suggest that analyzing the contributions of individual observations to the model's fit and complexity can lead to a diagnostic plot of deviance residuals against leverages. By incorporating pD into the posterior mean deviance, they formulate a deviance information criterion (DIC) for model comparison, which is related to other information criteria and has a decision-theoretic justification.

The methodology is illustrated through examples, and comparisons are made with other Bayesian and classical approaches. The authors highlight that the necessary computations can be easily performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods.

instances.input: Likelihood ratios are one of the best measures of diagnostic accuracy, although they are seldom used, because interpreting them requires a calculator to convert back and forth between “probability” and “odds” of disease. This article describes a simpler method of interpreting likelihood ratios, one that avoids calculators, nomograms, and conversions to “odds” of disease. Several examples illustrate how the clinician can use this method to refine diagnostic decisions at the bedside.

instances.input: On recognition of influenza virus (Flu) by TLR7, plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDCs) produce type I IFN in significant amounts. Synthetic TLR7 ligands induce the maturation of pDCs, as evidenced by the expression of costimulatory molecules and the production of proinflammatory cytokines; however, they induce only low-level production of IFN-alpha. To dissect the TLR7 signaling in pDCs and how these different profiles are induced, we studied the effects of 2 TLR7 ligands (Flu and CL097) on the activation of blood-isolated pDCs and the human GEN2.2 pDC cell line. Type I IFN production by pDCs correlates with differential interferon regulatory factor 7 (IRF7) translocation into the nucleus induced by the 2 TLR7 ligands. Surprisingly, with both activators we nevertheless observed the rapid expression of the IFN-inducible genes mxa, cxcl10, and trail within 4 hours of stimulation. This expression, controlled by STAT1 phosphorylation, was independent of type I IFN. STAT1 activation was found to be strictly dependent on the PI3K-p38MAPK pathway, showing a new signaling pathway leading to rapid expression of IFN-inducible genes after TLR7 triggering. Thus, pDCs, through this unusual TLR7 signaling, have the capacity to promptly respond to viral infection during the early phases of the innate immune response.
instances.output: induction of early IFN-inducible genes in the absence of type

The research reveals that the differential translocation of interferon regulatory factor 7 (IRF7) into the nucleus is associated with the varying levels of type I IFN production. Interestingly, both TLR7 ligands trigger the rapid expression of IFN-inducible genes such as mxa, cxcl10, and trail within 4 hours, independent of type I IFN, and this expression is regulated by STAT1 phosphorylation. The activation of STAT1 is shown to rely on the PI3K-p38MAPK signaling pathway, indicating a novel mechanism for the quick response of pDCs to viral infections during the early stages of the innate immune response.

instances.input:
OBJECTIVE The purpose of this study was to characterize the relationship between adipose tissue phenotype and depot-specific microvascular function in fat.   
 METHODS AND RESULTS In 30 obese subjects (age 42±11 years, body mass index 46±11 kg/m(2)) undergoing bariatric surgery, we intraoperatively collected visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue and characterized depot-specific adipose phenotypes. We assessed vasomotor function of the adipose microvasculature using videomicroscopy of small arterioles (75-250 μm) isolated from different fat compartments. Endothelium-dependent, acetylcholine-mediated vasodilation was severely impaired in visceral arterioles, compared to the subcutaneous depot (P<0.001 by ANOVA). Nonendothelium dependent responses to papaverine and nitroprusside were similar. Endothelial nitric oxide synthase inhibition with N(ω)-nitro-l-arginine methyl ester reduced subcutaneous vasodilation but had no effect on severely blunted visceral arteriolar responses. Visceral fat exhibited greater expression of proinflammatory, oxidative stress-related, hypoxia-induced, and proangiogenic genes; increased activated macrophage populations; and had a higher capacity for cytokine production ex vivo.   
 CONCLUSIONS Our findings provide clinical evidence that the visceral microenvironment may be intrinsically toxic to arterial health providing a potential mechanism by which visceral adiposity burden is linked to atherosclerotic vascular disease. Our findings also support the evolving concept that both adipose tissue quality and quantity may play significant roles in shaping cardiovascular phenotypes in human obesity.
instances.output: Arteriolar function in visceral adipose tissue is impaired in human obesity.
